title: A Multimodal and Explainable Machine Learning Approach to Diagnosing Multi-Class Ejection Fraction from Electrocardiograms
summary: Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) assessment depends on echocardiography, limiting access in primary care and resource-constrained settings. We developed a multimodal machine-learning framework that combines engineered 12-lead ECG timeseries features with structured EHR variables to classify LVEF into four clinically used strata: normal (>50%), mildly reduced (40-50%), moderately reduced (30-40%), and severely reduced (<30%). To support model explainability, we identified the most influential ECG and EHR features via SHAP attributions. Using retrospective data from Hartford HealthCare, we trained XGBoost models on 36,784 ECG-echocardiogram pairs from 30,952 outpatients and evaluated temporal generalizability on 19,966 ECGs from a subsequent period. The multimodal model achieved one-vs-rest AUROCs of 0.95 (severe), 0.92 (moderate), 0.82 (mild), and 0.91 (normal), outperforming ECG-only and EHR-only baselines, and maintained performance under temporal validation. This work supports ECG-based, multimodal LVEF stratification as a practical screening and triage aid to prioritize confirmatory imaging where resources are limited.

AI.tldr: Multimodal machine learning combining ECG and EHR data effectively classifies LVEF into clinical strata, outperforming single-modality models and offering a scalable screening tool for resource-limited settings.
AI.motivation: Current assessment of left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) relies heavily on echocardiography, which limits accessibility in primary care and resource-constrained settings due to cost, equipment, and expertise requirements. There is a need for a more accessible, cost-effective screening tool to stratify LVEF and prioritize patients for confirmatory imaging.
AI.method: The researchers developed a multimodal machine-learning framework using XGBoost models. They engineered features from 12-lead ECG timeseries and combined them with structured EHR variables to classify LVEF into four strata: normal (>50%), mildly reduced (40-50%), moderately reduced (30-40%), and severely reduced (<30%). They used SHAP attributions for model explainability to identify influential ECG and EHR features. Training was performed on retrospective data from Hartford HealthCare, specifically on 36,784 ECG-echocardiogram pairs from 30,952 outpatients, with temporal validation on 19,966 ECGs from a subsequent period.
AI.result: The multimodal model achieved one-vs-rest AUROCs of 0.95 for severe LVEF reduction, 0.92 for moderate, 0.82 for mild, and 0.91 for normal. It outperformed both ECG-only and EHR-only baseline models and maintained strong performance under temporal validation on a subsequent dataset of 19,966 ECGs.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a multimodal machine-learning framework combining 12-lead ECG timeseries features with structured EHR variables can effectively classify LVEF into four clinical strata, achieving high AUROCs, especially for severe and moderate reductions. This approach outperforms ECG-only and EHR-only models and shows temporal generalizability, supporting its use as a practical screening and triage tool in resource-constrained settings.

title: When Agents Shop for You: Role Coherence in AI-Mediated Markets
summary: Consumers are increasingly delegating purchase decisions to AI agents, providing natural-language descriptions of their preferences and identity. We argue that these representations constitute an information channel, role coherence, through which sellers can infer willingness to pay without explicit disclosure by the buyer agent, leading to preference leakage. In an experiment where a language-model buyer agent shops on behalf of a verbal consumer profile, we show that seller-side inference from dialogue alone recovers willingness to pay nearly one-for-one. Comparing this setting to a numeric-budget condition with confidentiality instructions cleanly isolates role coherence as distinct from instruction-following failure. Because this leakage arises from delegation itself, it cannot be mitigated at the prompt level. Instead, we propose architectural interventions that trade off personalization against preference privacy.

AI.tldr: AI shopping agents leak consumer preferences through natural language dialogue, enabling sellers to infer willingness to pay almost perfectly, requiring architectural solutions rather than prompt fixes.
AI.motivation: As consumers increasingly delegate purchases to AI agents using natural language descriptions, there's concern about "preference leakage" where sellers can infer willingness to pay from conversational patterns without explicit disclosure.
AI.method: Conducted experiments where language-model buyer agents shopped for consumers with verbal profiles. Compared two conditions: natural-language dialogue vs. numeric-budget with confidentiality instructions to isolate "role coherence" effects from instruction-following failures.
AI.result: Seller-side inference from dialogue alone recovered willingness to pay nearly one-for-one (almost perfect correlation). The leakage effect is distinct from instruction-following failure and arises from delegation architecture itself.
AI.conclusion: Preference leakage through AI shopping agents cannot be fixed at the prompt level since it's inherent to delegation. Requires architectural interventions that trade off personalization against privacy protection.

title: A Randomized PDE Energy driven Iterative Framework for Efficient and Stable PDE Solutions
summary: Efficient and stable solution of partial differential equations (PDEs) is central to scientific and engineering applications, yet existing numerical solvers rely heavily on matrix based discretizations, while learning based methods require costly training and often suffer from limited generalization. In this work, we proposes a PDE energy driven framework that solves PDEs through physically constrained diffusion iterations, without relying on classical matrix based finite element assembly or data driven neural network training. The proposed method evolves arbitrary random initial fields through PDE energy driven implicit iterations combined with Gaussian smoothing, while strictly enforcing boundary conditions at each iteration. The proposed formulation is applied to representative one dimensional Poisson, Heat, and viscous Burgers equations, covering both steady state and transient problems. Numerical results demonstrate stable convergence to the unique physical solution from random initializations, with accurate resolution of sharp gradients and controlled Mean Squared Error (MSE) across a wide range of discretization parameters. Detailed comparisons with analytical solutions indicate that the framework achieves competitive accuracy and stability. Overall, the proposed framework provides a fast, flexible, and physically consistent alternative to traditional numerical solvers, offering a potential pathway for scalable PDE solutions in both research and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: A PDE energy driven framework that solves PDEs through physically constrained diffusion iterations without matrix discretization or neural network training, achieving stable convergence to physical solutions with accurate gradient resolution.
AI.motivation: Existing numerical solvers rely heavily on matrix-based discretizations which can be computationally intensive, while learning-based methods require costly training and suffer from limited generalization capabilities. There is a need for more efficient, flexible, and physically consistent alternatives for solving PDEs in scientific and engineering applications.
AI.method: The proposed method evolves arbitrary random initial fields through PDE energy driven implicit iterations combined with Gaussian smoothing, while strictly enforcing boundary conditions at each iteration. It avoids both classical finite element assembly and data-driven neural network training, instead using physically constrained diffusion iterations.
AI.result: The method demonstrates stable convergence to unique physical solutions from random initializations, with accurate resolution of sharp gradients and controlled Mean Squared Error across a wide range of discretization parameters. It successfully solves representative 1D PDEs (Poisson, Heat, and viscous Burgers equations) covering both steady state and transient problems, achieving competitive accuracy and stability compared to analytical solutions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a fast, flexible, and physically consistent alternative to traditional numerical solvers, offering a potential pathway for scalable PDE solutions in both research and engineering applications without the limitations of matrix-based discretizations or data-driven training requirements.

title: Preserving Disagreement: Architectural Heterogeneity and Coherence Validation in Multi-Agent Policy Simulation
comment: 14 pages, 7 tables, 120 deliberations across 2 policy scenarios
summary: Multi-agent deliberation systems using large language models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed for policy simulation, yet they suffer from artificial consensus: evaluator agents converge on the same option regardless of their assigned value perspectives. We present the AI Council, a three-phase deliberation framework, and conduct 120 deliberations across two policy scenarios to test two interventions. First, architectural heterogeneity (assigning a different 7-9B parameter model to each value perspective) significantly reduces first-choice concentration compared to a homogeneous baseline (child welfare: 70.9% to 46.1%, p < 0.001, r = 0.58; housing: 46.0% to 22.9%, p < 0.001, r = 0.50). This contrasts with accuracy-oriented multi-agent debate, where heterogeneity does not reduce convergence, suggesting model diversity operates differently when no objectively correct answer exists. Second, coherence validation (using a frontier model to assess whether each evaluator's reasoning is grounded in its assigned values) reveals a fidelity-diversity tradeoff: on a scenario with a dominant option, it further reduces concentration (46.1% to 40.8%, p = 0.004), but on a scenario with genuinely competitive options, it increases concentration (22.9% to 26.6%, p = 0.96) by amplifying high-coherence evaluators who cluster on one option. This tradeoff may be a general property of multi-agent systems employing quality weighting. We report negative results from three failed Delphi designs, demonstrate that 8B models exhibit binary rather than graded responses to counter-arguments, and propose the trustworthy tension rate as a diagnostic measure of small-model deliberation capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AI Council, a three-phase deliberation framework for policy simulation that addresses artificial consensus in LLM-based multi-agent systems, finding architectural heterogeneity reduces first-choice concentration but coherence validation creates a fidelity-diversity tradeoff.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent deliberation systems using LLMs suffer from "artificial consensus" where evaluator agents converge on the same policy option regardless of their assigned value perspectives, limiting their usefulness for policy simulation.
AI.method: The authors present the AI Council, a three-phase deliberation framework, and conduct 120 deliberations across two policy scenarios (child welfare and housing) to test two interventions: 1) architectural heterogeneity (assigning different 7-9B parameter models to each value perspective), and 2) coherence validation (using a frontier model to assess whether evaluators' reasoning is grounded in their assigned values).
AI.result: Architectural heterogeneity significantly reduced first-choice concentration (child welfare: 70.9% to 46.1%; housing: 46.0% to 22.9%). Coherence validation revealed a fidelity-diversity tradeoff: it reduced concentration on scenarios with dominant options but increased concentration on scenarios with competitive options by amplifying high-coherence evaluators. The paper also reports negative results from three failed Delphi designs and finds that 8B models exhibit binary rather than graded responses to counter-arguments.
AI.conclusion: Model diversity reduces artificial consensus in policy deliberation systems, operating differently from accuracy-oriented debate systems. Coherence validation creates a fidelity-diversity tradeoff that may be a general property of quality-weighted multi-agent systems. The trustworthy tension rate is proposed as a diagnostic for small-model deliberation capabilities.

title: A Survey of Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning with Graph Neural Network-Based Communication
summary: In multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), the integration of a communication mechanism, allowing agents to better learn to coordinate their actions and converge on their objectives by sharing information. Based on an interaction graph, a subclass of methods employs graph neural networks (GNNs) to learn the communication, enabling agents to improve their internal representations by enriching them with information exchanged. With growing research, we note a lack of explicit structure and framework to distinguish and classify MARL approaches with communication based on GNNs. Thus, this paper surveys recent works in this field. We propose a generalized GNN-based communication process with the goal of making the underlying concepts behind the methods more obvious and accessible.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys and proposes a unified framework for classifying MARL approaches that use Graph Neural Networks for communication between agents.
AI.motivation: There's a growing body of research in multi-agent reinforcement learning using GNNs for communication, but no clear framework exists to distinguish and classify different approaches. The authors aim to bring structure to this emerging field.
AI.method: The paper conducts a comprehensive survey of recent works in MARL with GNN-based communication. The authors analyze existing methods and propose a generalized GNN-based communication process framework to organize and classify different approaches.
AI.result: The paper identifies a research gap in the systematic classification of MARL approaches using GNNs for communication and proposes a unified framework to make underlying concepts more accessible and comparable across different methods.
AI.conclusion: This survey provides a needed structure for the growing field of MARL with GNN-based communication, enabling better comparison, understanding, and future development of approaches in this area through a proposed generalized framework.

title: Rethinking KV Cache Eviction via a Unified Information-Theoretic Objective
summary: Key-value (KV) caching is essential for large language model inference, yet its memory overhead poses a critical bottleneck for long-context generation. Existing eviction policies predominantly rely on empirical heuristics, lacking a rigorous theoretical foundation. This work rethinks KV cache eviction through the lens of the Information Bottleneck principle. Under a linear-Gaussian surrogate of attention, we derive a closed-form mutual information objective that characterizes the effective information capacity of a retained KV cache subset. This formulation reveals that a wide range of existing eviction strategies can be interpreted as different approximations of the same capacity-maximization principle. Guided by this insight, we introduce CapKV, a capacity-aware eviction method that directly targets information preservation via a log-determinant approximation using statistical leverage scores. This approach replaces heuristic selection with a theoretically grounded mechanism that preserves the maximum predictive signal. Extensive experiments across multiple models and long-context benchmarks show that CapKV consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving a better trade-off between memory efficiency and generational fidelity.

AI.tldr: CapKV rethinks KV cache eviction using Information Bottleneck theory, deriving a capacity-maximization objective and implementing it via leverage scores, outperforming heuristic methods in memory-quality trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing KV cache eviction policies rely on empirical heuristics without theoretical foundation, creating a critical bottleneck for long-context generation due to memory overhead. There is a need for a rigorous theoretical approach to optimize KV cache management.
AI.method: The authors derive a closed-form mutual information objective under a linear-Gaussian surrogate of attention to characterize the effective information capacity of KV cache subsets. They then introduce CapKV, which uses a log-determinant approximation with statistical leverage scores to directly target information preservation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple models and long-context benchmarks show that CapKV consistently outperforms prior eviction methods, achieving better memory efficiency while preserving generation fidelity.
AI.conclusion: CapKV, a capacity-aware eviction method based on the Information Bottleneck principle, provides a theoretically grounded approach to KV cache eviction that consistently outperforms existing heuristic methods, offering a superior trade-off between memory efficiency and generation quality.

title: Operating-Layer Controls for Onchain Language-Model Agents Under Real Capital
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures. Public onchain dashboard and supporting documentation linked in paper
summary: We study reliability in autonomous language-model agents that translate user mandates into validated tool actions under real capital. The setting is DX Terminal Pro, a 21-day deployment in which 3,505 user-funded agents traded real ETH in a bounded onchain market. Users configured vaults through structured controls and natural-language strategies, but only agents could choose normal buy/sell trades. The system produced 7.5M agent invocations, roughly 300K onchain actions, about $20M in volume, more than 5,000 ETH deployed, roughly 70B inference tokens, and 99.9% settlement success for policy-valid submitted transactions. Long-running agents accumulated thousands of sequential decisions, including 6,000+ prompt-state-action cycles for continuously active agents, yielding a large-scale trace from user mandate to rendered prompt, reasoning, validation, portfolio state, and settlement. Reliability did not come from the base model alone; it emerged from the operating layer around the model: prompt compilation, typed controls, policy validation, execution guards, memory design, and trace-level observability. Pre-launch testing exposed failures that text-only benchmarks rarely measure, including fabricated trading rules, fee paralysis, numeric anchoring, cadence trading, and misread tokenomics. Targeted harness changes reduced fabricated sell rules from 57% to 3%, reduced fee-led observations from 32.5% to below 10%, and increased capital deployment from 42.9% to 78.0% in an affected test population. We show that capital-managing agents should be evaluated across the full path from user mandate to prompt, validated action, and settlement.

AI.tldr: Autonomous language-model agents trading real ETH achieved 99.9% settlement success rate through a comprehensive operating layer system, not just the base model alone, in a large-scale 21-day deployment with $20M volume.
AI.motivation: To study reliability in autonomous language-model agents that manage real capital by translating user mandates into validated tool actions, moving beyond text-only benchmarks to real-world deployment.
AI.method: Deployed 3,505 user-funded agents in DX Terminal Pro for 21 days to trade real ETH in a bounded onchain market. Users configured vaults with structured controls and natural-language strategies, while agents executed buy/sell trades. System tracked 7.5M agent invocations, 300K onchain actions.
AI.result: Achieved 99.9% settlement success rate, processed $20M volume, deployed 5,000+ ETH, used 70B inference tokens. Through targeted improvements, reduced fabricated sell rules from 57% to 3%, reduced fee-led observations from 32.5% to below 10%, increased capital deployment from 42.9% to 78.0%.
AI.conclusion: Reliability in capital-managing agents emerges from the operating layer (prompt compilation, typed controls, policy validation, execution guards, memory design, trace-level observability) not just the base model. Full-path evaluation from user mandate to settlement is crucial for real-world agent deployment.

title: Mini-Batch Class Composition Bias in Link Prediction
comment: Accepted at GCLR 2026: the 5th Workshop on Graphs and more Complex Structures For Learning and Reasoning, colocated with AAAI 2026
summary: Prior work on node classification has shown that Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) can learn representations that transfer across graphs, when underlying graph properties are shared. For a fixed graph, one would then expect GNNs trained for link prediction to learn a representation consistent with that learnt for node classification. We show this intuition does not hold in the general case. Instead, we find popular link prediction models can learn a trivial mini-batch dependent heuristic, enabled by batch-normalisation layers, to solve the edge classification task. When correcting for this, we observe increased alignment of the network representation with node-class relevant features, suggesting the network has learnt a graph representation that better aligns with the underlying graph's properties. Our findings suggest that standard link prediction training may be leading us to overestimate link predictors' ability to learn a generalised representation of a graph that is consistent across tasks.

AI.tldr: Popular link prediction GNNs can learn trivial batch-dependent heuristics instead of meaningful graph representations, potentially overestimating their generalization ability.
AI.motivation: While GNNs for node classification show transferable representations across graphs when properties are shared, the authors question whether link prediction GNNs similarly learn consistent representations that align with node-class relevant features for a fixed graph.
AI.method: The study investigates popular link prediction models and identifies that they can exploit batch-normalization layers to learn trivial mini-batch dependent heuristics to solve edge classification, rather than learning meaningful graph representations. They correct for this issue and examine the alignment of resulting network representations with node-class relevant features.
AI.result: When correcting for the trivial heuristic enabled by batch-normalization, the network representations show increased alignment with node-class relevant features, indicating better learning of underlying graph properties. This suggests standard link prediction training may overestimate models' ability to learn generalized graph representations consistent across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Current link prediction evaluation may be misleading, as models can learn trivial batch-dependent shortcuts rather than meaningful graph representations. Correcting for these artifacts reveals better alignment with graph properties and highlights the need for more rigorous evaluation of learned representations in link prediction tasks.

title: AGEL-Comp: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Compositional Generalization in Interactive Agents
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents exhibit systemic failures in compositional generalization, limiting their robustness in interactive environments. This work introduces AGEL-Comp, a neuro-symbolic AI agent architecture designed to address this challenge by grounding actions of the agent. AGEL-Comp integrates three core innovations: (1) a dynamic Causal Program Graph (CPG) as a world model, representing procedural and causal knowledge as a directed hypergraph; (2) an Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) engine that synthesizes new Horn clauses from experiential feedback, grounding symbolic knowledge through interaction; and (3) a hybrid reasoning core where an LLM proposes a set of candidate sub-goals that are verified for logical consistency by a Neural Theorem Prover (NTP). Together, these components operationalize a deduction--abduction learning cycle: enabling the agent to deduce plans and abductively expand its symbolic world model, while a neural adaptation phase keeps its reasoning engine aligned with new knowledge. We propose an evaluation protocol within the \texttt{Retro Quest} simulation environment to probe for compositional generalization scenarios to evaluate our AGEL agent. Our findings clearly indicate the better performance of our AGEL model over pure LLM-based models. Our framework presents a principled path toward agents that build an explicit, interpretable, and compositionally structured understanding of their world.

AI.tldr: AGEL-Comp is a neuro-symbolic AI agent architecture that integrates symbolic reasoning with LLMs to address compositional generalization failures in interactive environments through a deduction-abduction learning cycle.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents exhibit systemic failures in compositional generalization, limiting their robustness in interactive environments. There's a need for agents that can build explicit, interpretable, and compositionally structured understanding of their world.
AI.method: AGEL-Comp integrates three core innovations: (1) dynamic Causal Program Graph as a world model, (2) Inductive Logic Programming engine for synthesizing new Horn clauses from experiential feedback, and (3) hybrid reasoning core where LLM proposes candidate sub-goals verified by Neural Theorem Prover. This enables a deduction-abduction learning cycle with neural adaptation.
AI.result: Evaluation in the Retro Quest simulation environment shows AGEL-Comp performs better than pure LLM-based models in compositional generalization scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework presents a principled path toward agents that build explicit, interpretable, and compositionally structured understanding of their world, addressing key limitations of LLM-based agents in interactive environments.

title: Distill-Belief: Closed-Loop Inverse Source Localization and Characterization in Physical Fields
summary: {Closed-loop inverse source localization and characterization (ISLC) requires a mobile agent to select measurements that localize sources and infer latent field parameters under strict time constraints.} {The core challenge lies in the belief-space objective: valid uncertainty estimation requires expensive Bayesian inference, whereas using fast learned belief model leads to reward hacking, in which the policy exploits approximation errors rather than actually reducing uncertainty.} {We propose \textbf{Distill-Belief}, a teacher--student framework that decouples correctness from efficiency. A Bayes-correct particle-filter teacher maintains the posterior and supplies a dense information-gain signal, while a compact student distills the posterior into belief statistics for control and an uncertainty certificate for stopping. At deployment, only the student is used, yielding constant per-step cost.} {Experiments on seven field modalities and two stress tests show that Distill-Belief consistently reduces sensing cost and improves success, posterior contraction, and estimation accuracy over baselines, while mitigating reward hacking.}

AI.tldr: A teacher-student framework that separates Bayesian correctness from computational efficiency to solve closed-loop inverse source localization under time constraints while preventing reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Closed-loop inverse source localization requires mobile agents to localize sources and infer field parameters under strict time constraints, but Bayesian inference is computationally expensive while learned approximations lead to reward hacking where policies exploit model errors instead of actually reducing uncertainty.
AI.method: Distill-Belief uses a teacher-student framework where a Bayes-correct particle filter teacher maintains accurate posteriors and provides dense information-gain signals, while a compact student distills posterior into belief statistics for control and uncertainty certificates for stopping decisions.
AI.result: Experiments across seven field modalities and two stress tests show Distill-Belief consistently reduces sensing costs, improves success rates, posterior contraction, and estimation accuracy compared to baselines, while effectively mitigating reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Distill-Belief framework successfully decouples correctness from efficiency in closed-loop ISLC, enabling constant per-step computational cost during deployment while maintaining Bayesian accuracy and preventing reward hacking.

title: Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management
summary: Frontier AI both amplifies existing risks and introduces qualitatively novel challenges. Not only is there a notable lack of stable scientific consensus resulting from the rapid pace of technological change, but emerging frontier AI safety practices are often misaligned with, or may undermine, established risk management frameworks. To address these challenges, we systematically surface open problems in frontier AI risk management. Adopting a problem-oriented approach, we examine each stage of the risk management process - risk planning, identification, analysis, evaluation, and mitigation - through a structured review of the literature, identifying unresolved challenges and the actors best positioned to address them. Recognising that different types of open problems call for different responses, we classify open problems according to whether they reflect (a) a lack of scientific or technical consensus, (b) misalignment with, or challenges to, established risk management frameworks, or (c) shortcomings in implementation despite apparent consensus and alignment. By mapping these open problems and identifying the actors best positioned to address them - including developers, deployers, regulators, standards bodies, researchers, and third-party evaluators - this work aims to clarify where progress is needed to enable robust and meaningful consensus on frontier AI risk management.The paper does not propose specific solutions; instead, it provides a problem-oriented, agenda-setting reference document, complemented by a living online repository, intended to support coordination, reduce duplication, and guide future research and governance efforts.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically identifies and categorizes open problems in frontier AI risk management, mapping them to relevant stakeholders without proposing specific solutions, serving as an agenda-setting reference document.
AI.motivation: Frontier AI amplifies existing risks and creates novel challenges. There's a lack of stable scientific consensus due to rapid technological change, and emerging AI safety practices often misalign with or undermine established risk management frameworks.
AI.method: Adopting a problem-oriented approach, the authors examine each stage of the risk management process (planning, identification, analysis, evaluation, mitigation) through structured literature review. They identify unresolved challenges and classify open problems into three categories: lack of consensus, misalignment with frameworks, or implementation shortcomings.
AI.result: The paper maps open problems to relevant actors (developers, deployers, regulators, standards bodies, researchers, third-party evaluators) who are best positioned to address them. It provides a problem-oriented, agenda-setting reference document complemented by a living online repository.
AI.conclusion: The work aims to clarify where progress is needed to enable robust consensus on frontier AI risk management. It serves as a coordination tool to reduce duplication and guide future research and governance efforts without proposing specific solutions.

title: Bian Que: An Agentic Framework with Flexible Skill Arrangement for Online System Operations
summary: Operating and maintaining (O&M) large-scale online engine systems (search, recommendation, advertising) demands substantial human effort for release monitoring, alert response, and root cause analysis. While LLM-based agents are a natural fit for these tasks, the deployment bottleneck is not reasoning capability but orchestration: selecting, for each operational event, the relevant data (metrics, logs, change events) and the applicable operational knowledge (handbook rules and practitioner experience). Feeding all signals indiscriminately causes dilution and hallucination, while manually curating the event-to-(data, knowledge) mapping is intractable under dozens of daily releases. We present Bian Que, an agentic framework with three contributions: (i) a \emph{unified operational paradigm} abstracting day-to-day O&M into three canonical patterns: release interception, proactive inspection, and alert root cause analysis; (ii) \emph{Flexible Skill Arrangement}, where each Skill specifies which data and knowledge to retrieve for a given business-module context and can be automatically generated and updated by LLMs or iteratively refined through natural-language instructions from on-call engineers; (iii) a \emph{unified self-evolving mechanism} in which one correction signal drives two parallel pathways, case-memory-to-knowledge distillation and targeted Skill refinement. Deployed on the e-commerce search engine of KuaiShou, the major short-video platform in China, Bian Que reduces alert volume by 75%, achieves 80% root-cause analysis accuracy, and cuts mean time to resolution by over 50%. Our framework achieves 99.0% pass rate on offline evaluations. Our code is available at https://github.com/benchen4395/BianQue_Assistant.

AI.tldr: Bian Que is an LLM-based agentic framework for automating O&M tasks in large-scale online systems that reduces alert volume by 75% and improves root cause analysis accuracy through unified operational patterns and self-evolving skill orchestration.
AI.motivation: Operating and maintaining large-scale online engine systems (search, recommendation, advertising) requires substantial human effort for release monitoring, alert response, and root cause analysis. The main deployment bottleneck for LLM-based agents is orchestration - selecting relevant data and applicable operational knowledge for each event, as feeding all signals indiscriminately causes dilution and hallucination.
AI.method: Bian Que presents three key contributions: (1) A unified operational paradigm abstracting O&M into three canonical patterns: release interception, proactive inspection, and alert root cause analysis. (2) Flexible Skill Arrangement where Skills specify which data/knowledge to retrieve for given business contexts, automatically generated/updated by LLMs or refined through engineer instructions. (3) A unified self-evolving mechanism where correction signals drive parallel pathways for case-memory-to-knowledge distillation and targeted Skill refinement.
AI.result: Deployed on KuaiShou's e-commerce search engine, Bian Que reduces alert volume by 75%, achieves 80% root-cause analysis accuracy, and cuts mean time to resolution by over 50%. The framework achieves 99.0% pass rate on offline evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Bian Que effectively addresses the orchestration bottleneck in LLM-based O&M automation through its unified operational paradigm, flexible skill arrangement, and self-evolving mechanism, demonstrating significant operational improvements in real-world deployment at KuaiShou.

title: Evaluating Strategic Reasoning in Forecasting Agents
summary: Forecasting benchmarks produce accuracy leaderboards but little insight into why some forecasters are more accurate than others. We introduce Bench to the Future 2 (BTF-2), 1,417 pastcasting questions with a frozen 15M-document research corpus in which agents reproducibly research and forecast offline, producing full reasoning traces. BTF-2 detects accuracy differences of 0.004 Brier score, and can distinguish differential agent strengths in research vs. judgment. We build a forecaster 0.011 Brier more accurate than any single frontier agent, and use it to evaluate agent strategic reasoning without hindsight bias. We find the better forecaster differs primarily in its pre-mortem analysis of its blind spots and consideration of black swans. Expert human forecasters found the dominant strategic reasoning failures of frontier agents are in assessing political and business leaders' incentives, judging their likelihood to follow through on stated plans, and modeling institutional processes.

AI.tldr: BTF-2 is a large-scale pastcasting benchmark that enables reproducible research and forecasting with full reasoning traces, allowing for detailed analysis of why some forecasters perform better than others.
AI.motivation: Current forecasting benchmarks produce accuracy leaderboards but fail to provide insights into why certain forecasters are more accurate than others. There's a need to understand the underlying reasons for differential performance in forecasting.
AI.method: The authors created Bench to the Future 2 (BTF-2), which includes 1,417 pastcasting questions with a frozen 15M-document research corpus. Agents can reproducibly research and forecast offline while producing full reasoning traces, allowing detailed analysis of their processes.
AI.result: BTF-2 can detect accuracy differences as small as 0.004 Brier score and distinguish between agents' strengths in research vs. judgment. The researchers built a forecaster that was 0.011 Brier more accurate than any single frontier agent and used it to evaluate strategic reasoning without hindsight bias.
AI.conclusion: The superior forecaster's advantage primarily comes from better pre-mortem analysis of blind spots and consideration of black swans. Expert human forecasters identified that frontier agents' main strategic reasoning failures involve: assessing political/business leaders' incentives, judging follow-through on stated plans, and modeling institutional processes.

title: Correcting Performance Estimation Bias in Imbalanced Classification with Minority Subconcepts
summary: Class-level evaluation can conceal substantial performance disparities across subconcepts within the same class, causing models that perform well on average to fail on specific subpopulations. Prior work has shown that common evaluation measures for imbalanced classification are biased toward larger minority subconcepts and that utility-based reweighting using true subconcept labels can mitigate this bias; however, such labels are rarely available at test time. We introduce a practical utility-weighted evaluation that replaces unavailable subconcept labels with predicted posterior probabilities from a multiclass subconcept model. Evaluation weights are defined as the expected utility under this posterior, yielding a soft, uncertainty-aware metric we call predicted-weighted balanced accuracy (pBA). Experiments on tabular benchmarks as well as medical-imaging and text datasets show that unweighted scores can be misleading under within-class heterogeneity, while pBA provides more stable and interpretable assessments when subconcept distributions are uneven but not pathological. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/correcting-bias-imbalance-9C6C/.

AI.tldr: Introduces predicted-weighted balanced accuracy (pBA) for more accurate evaluation of models under within-class heterogeneity when subconcept labels are unavailable at test time.
AI.motivation: Standard class-level evaluation can hide performance disparities across subconcepts within the same class, causing models to fail on specific subpopulations despite good average performance.
AI.method: Proposes pBA (predicted-weighted balanced accuracy) which replaces unavailable true subconcept labels with predicted posterior probabilities from a multiclass subconcept model, defining evaluation weights as expected utility under this posterior.
AI.result: Experiments on tabular, medical-imaging, and text datasets show that unweighted scores can be misleading under within-class heterogeneity, while pBA provides more stable and interpretable assessments when subconcept distributions are uneven but not pathological.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pBA method offers a practical, uncertainty-aware evaluation metric that corrects bias toward larger minority subconcepts when subconcept labels are unavailable at test time, enabling more reliable model assessment under within-class heterogeneity.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Persona Induction from User Behavioral Logs: Learning Evidence-Grounded and Truthful Personas
summary: Behavioral logs provide rich signals for user modeling, but are noisy and interleaved across diverse intents. Recent work uses LLMs to generate interpretable natural-language personas from user logs, yet evaluation often emphasizes downstream utility, providing limited assurance of persona quality itself. We propose a hierarchical framework that aggregates user actions into intent memories and induces multiple evidence-grounded personas by clustering and labeling these memories. We formulate persona induction as an optimization problem over persona quality-captured by cluster cohesion, persona-evidence alignment, and persona truthfulness-and train the persona model using a groupwise extension of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Experiments on a large-scale service log and two public datasets show that our method induces more coherent, evidence-grounded, and trustworthy personas, while also improving future interaction prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a hierarchical framework that aggregates user behavioral logs into intent memories, then clusters and labels these memories to generate evidence-grounded personas. It formulates persona induction as an optimization problem and uses a groupwise extension of DPO for training.
AI.motivation: Behavioral logs provide rich signals for user modeling but are noisy and interleaved across diverse intents. Current LLM-based methods for generating interpretable personas from logs focus on downstream utility with limited assurance of persona quality itself.
AI.method: Hierarchical framework that: 1) aggregates user actions into intent memories, 2) induces multiple evidence-grounded personas by clustering and labeling these memories. Formulates persona induction as optimization problem capturing cluster cohesion, persona-evidence alignment, and persona truthfulness. Trains persona model using groupwise extension of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).
AI.result: Experiments on a large-scale service log and two public datasets show that the method induces more coherent, evidence-grounded, and trustworthy personas compared to baseline approaches. Also improves future interaction prediction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical framework effectively generates higher quality personas from behavioral logs by explicitly optimizing for persona quality dimensions (cohesion, alignment, truthfulness) rather than just downstream utility, while maintaining practical utility for prediction tasks.

title: RaMP: Runtime-Aware Megakernel Polymorphism for Mixture-of-Experts
summary: The optimal kernel configuration for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inference depends on both batch size and the expert routing distribution, yet production systems dispatch from batch size alone, leaving 10-70% of kernel throughput unrealized. We present RaMP, a routing-aware dispatch framework. A performance-region analysis derives, from hardware constants alone, when each optimization helps, correctly predicting all 8 tested architectures, including 3 unseen. A four-parameter wave cost model selects the fastest configuration from the runtime expert histogram, achieving 0.93% mean regret versus exhaustive search, fitted from just 10-24 minutes of one-time profiling per model. Because the model depends only on CTA grid geometry, it is kernel-agnostic: applied to Alpha-MoE, it delivers 1.14x with no source modification. Paired with a co-designed CuTe DSL kernel exposing 134-268 polymorphic configurations, RaMP delivers 1.22x kernel speedup over static dispatch and 1.30x end-to-end speedup in vLLM serving over Triton, 1.41x over DeepGEMM, and 1.13x over FlashInfer CUTLASS.

AI.tldr: RaMP is a routing-aware dispatch framework for Mixture-of-Experts inference that dynamically selects optimal kernel configurations based on runtime expert routing patterns, achieving significant speedups over static dispatch methods.
AI.motivation: Current production systems dispatch MoE kernels based on batch size alone, ignoring expert routing distribution, which leaves 10-70% of kernel throughput unrealized. This suboptimal performance occurs because the ideal kernel configuration depends on both batch size and the expert routing distribution.
AI.method: 1) A performance-region analysis derived from hardware constants alone predicts when each optimization helps across different architectures. 2) A four-parameter wave cost model selects the fastest configuration from runtime expert histograms. 3) Paired with a co-designed CuTe DSL kernel that exposes 134-268 polymorphic configurations. 4) The approach is kernel-agnostic and can be applied to existing kernels without modification.
AI.result: The framework achieves 0.93% mean regret versus exhaustive search with only 10-24 minutes of one-time profiling per model. Applied to Alpha-MoE, it delivers 1.14x speedup with no source modification. Combined with custom kernels, RaMP delivers 1.22x kernel speedup over static dispatch and 1.30x end-to-end speedup in vLLM serving over Triton, 1.41x over DeepGEMM, and 1.13x over FlashInfer CUTLASS.
AI.conclusion: RaMP demonstrates that routing-aware dispatch is crucial for optimal MoE inference performance, with a practical, kernel-agnostic approach that significantly outperforms current static dispatch methods across various hardware architectures and serving frameworks.

title: OMEGA: Optimizing Machine Learning by Evaluating Generated Algorithms
comment: ICLR 2026: Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement
summary: In order to automate AI research we introduce a full, end-to-end framework, OMEGA: Optimizing Machine learning by Evaluating Generated Algorithms, that starts at idea generation and ends with executable code. Our system combines structured meta-prompt engineering with executable code generation to create new ML classifiers. The OMEGA framework has been utilized to generate several novel algorithms that outperform scikit-learn baselines across a robust selection of 20 benchmark datasets (infinity-bench). You can access models discussed in this paper and more in the python package: pip install omega-models.

AI.tldr: OMEGA is an end-to-end framework that automates AI research by generating new machine learning algorithms through meta-prompt engineering and code generation, creating novel classifiers that outperform existing baselines.
AI.motivation: To automate AI research and reduce manual effort in developing new machine learning algorithms by creating a system that can generate novel algorithms from idea generation to executable code.
AI.method: Combines structured meta-prompt engineering with executable code generation to create new ML classifiers in a full end-to-end framework.
AI.result: Generated several novel algorithms that outperform scikit-learn baselines across 20 benchmark datasets (infinity-bench). The system is available as a Python package: omega-models.
AI.conclusion: OMEGA successfully demonstrates automated AI research by creating novel, effective machine learning algorithms through meta-prompt engineering and code generation, providing a practical framework for algorithm discovery.

title: Observable Neural ODEs for Identifiable Causal Forecasting in Continuous Time
summary: Causal inference in continuous-time sequential decision problems is challenged by hidden confounders. We show that, in latent state-space models with time-varying interventions, observability of the latent dynamics from observed data is necessary for identifying dynamic treatment effects, linking control-theoretic observability to causal identifiability, even when hidden confounders affect both treatments and outcomes. We derive a continuous-time adjustment formula expressing potential outcome distributions under treatment trajectories via the measurement model, latent dynamics, and the filtering distribution over latent states given observed histories. We propose Observable Neural ODEs (ObsNODEs), Neural ODE models in observable normal form for causal forecasting. ObsNODEs learn continuous-time dynamics with states reconstructible from observations, enabling outcome prediction under alternative treatment paths. Experiments on synthetic cancer data, semi-synthetic data based on MIMIC-IV, and real-world sepsis data show strong performance over recent sequence models.

AI.tldr: Continuous-time causal inference with hidden confounders requires observability of latent dynamics; Observable Neural ODEs enable treatment effect identification and outcome prediction under alternative treatment trajectories.
AI.motivation: Causal inference in continuous-time sequential decision problems faces challenges from hidden confounders that affect both treatments and outcomes, making it difficult to identify dynamic treatment effects.
AI.method: Proposes Observable Neural ODEs (ObsNODEs) - Neural ODE models in observable normal form that learn continuous-time dynamics where states are reconstructible from observations, linking control-theoretic observability to causal identifiability and deriving a continuous-time adjustment formula.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic cancer data, semi-synthetic data based on MIMIC-IV, and real-world sepsis data demonstrate strong performance over recent sequence models, showing ObsNODEs can effectively predict outcomes under alternative treatment paths.
AI.conclusion: Observability of latent dynamics is necessary for identifying dynamic treatment effects in continuous-time settings with hidden confounders, and ObsNODEs provide a practical framework for causal forecasting in such scenarios.

title: Persuadability and LLMs as Legal Decision Tools
comment: Accepted to 21st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Law (ICAIL 2026)
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are proposed as legal decision assistants, and even first-instance decision-makers, across a range of judicial and administrative contexts, it becomes essential to explore how they answer legal questions, and in particular the factors that lead them to decide difficult questions in one way or another. A specific feature of legal decisions is the need to respond to arguments advanced by contending parties. A legal decision-maker must be able to engage with, and respond to, including through being potentially persuaded by, arguments advanced by the parties. Conversely, they should not be unduly persuadable, influenced by a particularly compelling advocate to decide cases based on the skills of the advocates, rather than the merits of the case. We explore how frontier open- and closed-weights LLMs respond to legal arguments, reporting original experimental results examining how the quality of the advocate making those arguments affects the likelihood that a model will agree with a particular legal point of view, and exploring the factors driving these results. Our results have implications for the feasibility of adopting LLMs across legal and administrative settings.

AI.tldr: LLMs as legal decision-makers show significant sensitivity to argument quality, with better-written arguments increasing agreement likelihood by up to 13.4% for closed models and 35.7% for open models.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly proposed for legal decision-making roles, it's crucial to understand how they respond to legal arguments and whether they are unduly influenced by advocacy quality rather than case merits.
AI.method: Experimental examination of frontier open- and closed-weights LLMs' responses to legal arguments, testing how advocate quality affects model agreement with legal viewpoints and analyzing underlying factors.
AI.result: Both open and closed models show significant sensitivity to argument quality - better-written arguments increase likelihood of agreement by up to 13.4% for closed models and 35.7% for open models.
AI.conclusion: LLMs' susceptibility to argument quality raises concerns about their suitability for legal decision-making, as they may be influenced by advocacy skills rather than case merits, affecting feasibility of adoption in legal settings.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework for Distributed Chemical Process Optimization
summary: Industrial chemical plants often operate under strict data confidentiality constraints, making centralized data-driven process modeling difficult. Federated learning (FL) provides a promising solution by enabling collaborative model training across distributed facilities without sharing raw operational data. This paper proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning framework for distributed chemical process optimization using data collected from multiple geographically separated plants. Each plant locally trains a neural-network-based process model using its own time-series sensor data, while only model parameters are transmitted to a central aggregation server through secure aggregation mechanisms. This design allows cross-plant knowledge sharing while maintaining strict data locality and industrial confidentiality. Experimental evaluation was conducted using process datasets from three independent chemical plants operating under heterogeneous conditions. The results demonstrate rapid convergence of the federated model, with the global mean squared error decreasing from approximately 2369 to below 50 within the first five communication rounds and stabilizing around 35 after 40 rounds. In comparison with local-only training, the proposed federated framework significantly improves prediction accuracy across all plants, while achieving performance comparable to centralized training. The findings indicate that federated learning provides an effective and scalable solution for collaborative industrial analytics, enabling privacy-preserving predictive modeling and process optimization across distributed chemical production facilities.

AI.tldr: A privacy-preserving federated learning framework for distributed chemical process optimization that enables collaborative model training across multiple plants without sharing raw data, achieving performance comparable to centralized training while maintaining strict data confidentiality.
AI.motivation: Chemical plants operate under strict data confidentiality constraints that prevent centralized data sharing, making traditional data-driven process modeling difficult. There's a need for collaborative analytics across distributed facilities while maintaining industrial confidentiality and data privacy.
AI.method: Proposes a federated learning framework where each plant locally trains neural-network-based process models using its own time-series sensor data. Only model parameters are transmitted to a central server through secure aggregation mechanisms, enabling cross-plant knowledge sharing while maintaining data locality.
AI.result: The federated model converges rapidly, with global mean squared error decreasing from ~2369 to below 50 within first 5 communication rounds and stabilizing around 35 after 40 rounds. Significantly improves prediction accuracy compared to local-only training and achieves performance comparable to centralized training across all three plants operating under heterogeneous conditions.
AI.conclusion: Federated learning provides an effective and scalable solution for collaborative industrial analytics, enabling privacy-preserving predictive modeling and process optimization across distributed chemical production facilities while maintaining strict data confidentiality.

title: Apriori-based Analysis of Learned Helplessness in Mathematics Tutoring: Behavioral Patterns by Level, Intervention, and Outcome
summary: This study applied the Apriori algorithm to analyze behavioral interaction patterns associated with learned helplessness (LH) in mathematics tutoring system logs. Interaction data were examined across three dimensions: LH level (low vs. high), system-based intervention (with vs. without), and problem-solving outcomes (solved vs. unsolved). The analysis of the complete dataset showed that skipping problems without using hints was the most frequent pattern linked to unsolved outcomes, while persistence behaviors such as not skipping were less dominant overall. Comparisons by LH level showed that low-LH students had stronger links between problem solving and not skipping, as well as positive associations between hint use and solved outcomes. High-LH students showed more avoidance patterns, with skipping strongly tied to unsolved outcomes. In the comparison of system-based intervention conditions, students without intervention had the highest lift for persistence-success links, while the with-intervention group had stronger patterns involving skipping behaviors leading to unsolved outcomes. Outcome-specific analysis showed that not skipping was consistently associated with solved problems across all groups, while skipping without hints predicted unsolved outcomes. Practical implications and recommendations are discussed.

AI.tldr: The study uses Apriori algorithm to analyze math tutoring logs, finding that skipping problems without hints leads to unsolved outcomes, while persistence (not skipping) leads to solved problems, with differences between low and high learned helplessness students and intervention groups.
AI.motivation: To understand behavioral interaction patterns associated with learned helplessness (LH) in mathematics tutoring systems, particularly how different behaviors lead to problem-solving outcomes and how LH levels and system interventions affect these patterns.
AI.method: Applied Apriori algorithm to analyze tutoring system logs across three dimensions: LH level (low vs. high), system-based intervention (with vs. without), and problem-solving outcomes (solved vs. unsolved). Examined interaction data patterns and associations between behaviors and outcomes.
AI.result: Skipping problems without hints was the most frequent pattern linked to unsolved outcomes. Low-LH students showed stronger links between problem solving and not skipping, plus positive hint-solved associations. High-LH students showed more avoidance patterns with skipping-unsolved ties. Intervention group had stronger persistence-success links, while non-intervention group had stronger skipping-unsolved patterns. Not skipping consistently predicted solved problems across groups.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies clear behavioral patterns distinguishing successful vs. unsuccessful problem-solving in math tutoring, with LH levels moderating these relationships. Findings suggest tailored interventions could target different behavioral patterns for low vs. high LH students, with persistence behaviors being crucial for success across all groups.

title: PPG-Based Affect Recognition with Long-Range Deep Models: A Measurement-Driven Comparison of CNN, Transformer, and Mamba Architectures
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG) is increasingly used in wearable affective computing due to its low cost and ease of integration into consumer devices. Recent advances in deep learning have introduced long-range sequence models, such as Transformers, and state-space models, like Mamba, which have demonstrated strong performance on natural language and general time-series tasks. However, it remains unclear whether these architectures offer tangible benefits over widely used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTMs) for PPG-based affect recognition, given that datasets are typically small and noisy. This work presents a measurement-driven comparison of four deep learning architectures, CNN, CNN-LSTM hybrid, Transformers, and Mamba, for classifying arousal, valence, and relaxation states from wrist-based PPG signals. All models are evaluated under a subject-independent 5-fold cross-validation protocol using identical preprocessing, segmentation, and training pipelines. Our results show that the Transformer and Mamba models achieve performance comparable to that of a CNN baseline, but do not consistently outperform it across all tasks. CNNs remain the most effective overall, providing the highest accuracy with the smallest model size, whereas Transformers have a better balance of F1 scores for Arousal and Relaxation. The study provides the first evaluation of Transformer and Mamba models for PPG-based affect recognition, offering practical guidance on model selection for wearable affective monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: Transformers and Mamba models don't consistently outperform CNNs for PPG-based affect recognition; CNNs remain most effective overall with smallest model size.
AI.motivation: While Transformers and state-space models like Mamba show strong performance in NLP and general time-series tasks, it's unclear if they offer benefits over CNNs and LSTMs for PPG-based affect recognition given small, noisy datasets common in this domain.
AI.method: Comprehensive comparison of four deep learning architectures (CNN, CNN-LSTM hybrid, Transformers, Mamba) for classifying arousal, valence, and relaxation states from wrist-based PPG signals using subject-independent 5-fold cross-validation with identical preprocessing, segmentation, and training pipelines.
AI.result: Transformer and Mamba models achieve performance comparable to CNN baseline but don't consistently outperform it across all tasks. CNNs provide highest accuracy with smallest model size overall, while Transformers show better balance of F1 scores for Arousal and Relaxation.
AI.conclusion: For PPG-based affect recognition, CNNs remain the most practical choice due to their effectiveness and efficiency, though Transformers offer some advantages in specific tasks. This provides the first evaluation of Transformer and Mamba models in this domain with practical guidance for model selection in wearable systems.

title: DreamProver: Evolving Transferable Lemma Libraries via a Wake-Sleep Theorem-Proving Agent
summary: We introduce DreamProver, an agentic framework that leverages a "wake-sleep" program induction paradigm to discover reusable lemmas for formal theorem proving. Existing approaches either rely on fixed lemma libraries, which limit adaptability, or synthesize highly specific intermediate lemmas tailored to individual theorems, thereby lacking generality. DreamProver addresses this gap through an iterative two-stage process. In the wake stage, DreamProver attempts to prove theorems from a training set using the current lemma library while proposing new candidate lemmas. In the "sleep" stage, it abstracts, refines, and consolidates these candidates to compress and optimize the library. Through this alternating cycle, DreamProver progressively evolves a compact set of high-level, transferable lemmas that can be effectively used to prove unseen theorems in related domains. Experimental results demonstrate that DreamProver substantially improves proof success rates across a diverse set of mathematical benchmarks, while also producing more concise proofs and reducing computational cost.

AI.tldr: DreamProver uses wake-sleep program induction to discover reusable lemmas for formal theorem proving, improving success rates while producing concise proofs.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches either use fixed lemma libraries that lack adaptability or synthesize highly specific intermediate lemmas that lack generality, creating a gap for discovering reusable, transferable lemmas.
AI.method: DreamProver employs an iterative two-stage "wake-sleep" paradigm: 1) Wake stage: attempts to prove theorems using current lemma library while proposing new candidates; 2) Sleep stage: abstracts, refines, and consolidates candidates to optimize the library.
AI.result: Experimental results show DreamProver substantially improves proof success rates across diverse mathematical benchmarks while producing more concise proofs and reducing computational costs.
AI.conclusion: DreamProver's wake-sleep approach successfully evolves a compact set of high-level, transferable lemmas that can effectively prove unseen theorems in related domains, addressing limitations of existing methods.

title: Momentum-Conserving Graph Neural Networks for Deformable Objects
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a versatile and efficient option for modeling the dynamic behavior of deformable materials. While GNNs generalize readily to arbitrary shapes, mesh topologies, and material parameters, existing architectures struggle to correctly predict the temporal evolution of key physical quantities such as linear and angular momentum. In this work, we propose MomentumGNN -- a novel architecture designed to accurately track momentum by construction. Unlike existing GNNs that output unconstrained nodal accelerations, our model predicts per-edge stretching and bending impulses which guarantee the preservation of linear and angular momentum. We train our network in an unsupervised fashion using a physics-based loss, and we show that our method outperforms baselines in a number of common scenarios where momentum plays a pivotal role.

AI.tldr: MomentumGNN is a novel graph neural network architecture that preserves linear and angular momentum by construction, unlike existing GNNs that struggle with physical conservation laws.
AI.motivation: Existing GNN architectures for modeling deformable materials fail to correctly predict the temporal evolution of key physical quantities like linear and angular momentum, despite their flexibility in handling arbitrary shapes and mesh topologies.
AI.method: Proposes MomentumGNN which outputs per-edge stretching and bending impulses instead of unconstrained nodal accelerations, guaranteeing momentum preservation by construction. Trained using unsupervised learning with physics-based loss functions.
AI.result: The method outperforms baseline approaches in multiple scenarios where momentum conservation plays a crucial role, demonstrating accurate tracking of momentum evolution.
AI.conclusion: MomentumGNN represents a significant advancement in physics-informed machine learning for deformable materials by ensuring fundamental physical conservation laws are respected, enabling more accurate and physically plausible simulations.

title: Auto-Relational Reasoning
summary: Background & Objectives: In the last decade, Machine learning research has grown rapidly, but large models are reaching their soft limits demonstrating diminishing returns and still lack solid reasoning abilities. These limits could be surpassed through synergistic combination of Machine Learning scalability and rigid reasoning. Methods: In this work, we propose a theoretical framework for reasoning through object-relations in an automated manner integrated with Artificial Neural Networks. We present a formal analysis of the Reasoning, and we show the theory in practice through a paradigm integrating Reasoning and Machine Learning. Results: This paradigm is a system that solves Intelligence Quotient problems without any prior knowledge of the problem. Our system achieves 98.03% solving rate corresponding to the top 1% percentile or 132-144 iq score. This result is only limited by the small size of the model and the processing capabilities of the machine it run on. Conclusions: With the integration of prior knowledge in the system and the expansion of the dataset, the system can be generalized to solve a large category of problems. The functionality of the system inherently favors the solution of such problems in few-shot or zero-shot attempts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a theoretical framework that combines machine learning scalability with rigid reasoning to create a system that solves IQ problems without prior knowledge, achieving 98.03% solving rate.
AI.motivation: Large machine learning models are reaching soft limits with diminishing returns and lack solid reasoning abilities. The authors aim to surpass these limits through synergistic combination of machine learning scalability with rigid reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose a theoretical framework for reasoning through object-relations in an automated manner integrated with Artificial Neural Networks. They present a formal analysis of reasoning and demonstrate the theory through a paradigm integrating reasoning and machine learning.
AI.result: The developed system achieves 98.03% solving rate on Intelligence Quotient problems without any prior knowledge of the problems, corresponding to the top 1% percentile or 132-144 IQ score. This result is limited only by model size and processing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: With integration of prior knowledge and dataset expansion, the system can be generalized to solve a large category of problems. The system's functionality inherently favors solving such problems in few-shot or zero-shot attempts.

title: reward-lens: A Mechanistic Interpretability Library for Reward Models
comment: 30 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables, including appendix. Library available at https://github.com/suhailnadaf509/reward-lens (pip install reward-lens)
summary: Every RLHF-trained language model is shaped by a reward model, yet the mechanistic interpretability toolkit -- logit lens, direct logit attribution, activation patching, sparse autoencoders -- was built for generative LLMs whose primitives all project onto a vocabulary unembedding. Reward models replace that with a scalar regression head, breaking each tool. We present reward-lens, an open-source library that ports this toolkit to reward models, organised around one observation: the reward head's weight vector $w_r$ is the natural axis for every interpretability question. The library provides a Reward Lens, component attribution, three-mode activation patching, a reward-hacking probe suite, TopK SAE feature attribution, cross-model comparison, and five theory-grounded extensions (distortion index, divergence-aware patching, misalignment cascade detection, reward-term conflict analysis, concept-vector analysis). A ten-method adapter protocol covers Llama, Mistral, Gemma-2, and ArmoRM multi-objective heads, with a generic adapter for any HuggingFace sequence classification model. We validate on two production reward models across ~695 RewardBench pairs. The central empirical finding is negative: linear attribution does not predict causal patching effects (mean Spearman $ρ= -0.256$ on Skywork, $-0.027$ on ArmoRM). The framework treats this disagreement as a property to expose, not a bug -- motivating a design that keeps observational and causal views first-class and directly comparable.

AI.tldr: Reward-lens: A library that adapts mechanistic interpretability tools (logit lens, activation patching, etc.) to work with reward models by treating the reward head weight vector as the central axis for interpretation.
AI.motivation: Existing mechanistic interpretability tools were designed for generative language models with vocabulary unembedding projections, but reward models replace this with scalar regression heads, breaking all these tools. There's a need for interpretability methods specifically tailored to reward models.
AI.method: Created reward-lens library organized around the reward head's weight vector as the natural axis for interpretability. Includes Reward Lens, component attribution, three-mode activation patching, reward-hacking probe suite, TopK SAE feature attribution, cross-model comparison, and five theoretical extensions. Also developed ten-method adapter protocol for various model architectures.
AI.result: Key empirical finding: linear attribution does not predict causal patching effects (mean Spearman ρ = -0.256 on Skywork, -0.027 on ArmoRM). Framework validates on two production reward models across ~695 RewardBench pairs.
AI.conclusion: The reward-lens framework treats the disagreement between linear attribution and causal patching as a property to expose rather than a bug, maintaining both observational and causal views as first-class and directly comparable. Provides an open-source solution for mechanistic interpretability of reward models.

title: Grounding vs. Compositionality: On the Non-Complementarity of Reasoning in Neuro-Symbolic Systems
summary: Compositional generalization remains a foundational weakness of modern neural networks, limiting their robustness and applicability in domains requiring out-of-distribution reasoning. A central, yet unverified, assumption in neuro-symbolic AI is that compositional reasoning will emerge as a byproduct of successful symbol grounding. This work presents the first systematic empirical analysis to challenge this assumption by disentangling the contributions of grounding and reasoning. To operationalize this investigation, we introduce the Iterative Logic Tensor Network ($i$LTN), a fully differentiable architecture designed for multi-step deduction. Using a formal taxonomy of generalization -- probing for novel entities, unseen relations, and complex rule compositions -- we demonstrate that a model trained solely on a grounding objective fails to generalize. In contrast, our full $i$LTN, trained jointly on perceptual grounding and multi-step reasoning, achieves high zero-shot accuracy across all tasks. Our findings provide conclusive evidence that symbol grounding, while necessary, is insufficient for generalization, establishing that reasoning is not an emergent property but a distinct capability that requires an explicit learning objective.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that symbol grounding alone is insufficient for compositional generalization in neural networks, and that explicit reasoning objectives are required for zero-shot performance on novel entities, relations, and rule compositions.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the foundational weakness of compositional generalization in neural networks and challenges the unverified assumption in neuro-symbolic AI that compositional reasoning emerges as a byproduct of successful symbol grounding.
AI.method: The authors introduce Iterative Logic Tensor Network (iLTN), a fully differentiable architecture for multi-step deduction, and use a formal taxonomy of generalization to systematically analyze contributions of grounding vs. reasoning.
AI.result: Models trained only on grounding objectives fail to generalize, while the full iLTN trained jointly on perceptual grounding and multi-step reasoning achieves high zero-shot accuracy across all generalization tasks (novel entities, unseen relations, complex rule compositions).
AI.conclusion: Symbol grounding is necessary but insufficient for generalization; reasoning is not an emergent property but a distinct capability requiring explicit learning objectives. This provides evidence against the assumption in neuro-symbolic AI.

title: Spatially-constrained clustering of geospatial features for heat vulnerability assessment of favelas in Rio de Janeiro
summary: Informal settlements face disproportionate exposure to climate-related health hazards. However, existing methodologies lack systematic approaches to link diverse settlement characteristics with environmental health outcomes. We develop a data-driven framework to assess heat vulnerability in Rio de Janeiro's favelas by combining spatially-constrained clustering with land surface temperature (LST) analysis. Using remote sensing and geospatial features, we identify two distinct favela typologies: recent, well-connected settlements on flat terrain (Cluster 0) and historical, poorly-connected communities on vegetated slopes (Cluster 1). Analysis of 16 extreme heat events reveals systematic temperature differences of 2--3$^\circ$C between clusters, with flat-terrain favelas experiencing significantly higher heat exposure. Our findings demonstrate that settlement morphology critically influences heat vulnerability, providing a replicable framework for targeted urban planning and public health interventions in informal settlements globally.

AI.tldr: A data-driven framework combining spatial clustering and temperature analysis reveals how different favela morphologies in Rio de Janeiro create systematic heat vulnerability patterns, with flat-terrain settlements experiencing 2-3°C higher temperatures during extreme heat events.
AI.motivation: Informal settlements face disproportionate climate-related health hazards, but existing methodologies lack systematic approaches to link settlement characteristics with environmental health outcomes, particularly heat vulnerability.
AI.method: Developed a data-driven framework combining spatially-constrained clustering with land surface temperature (LST) analysis using remote sensing and geospatial features to assess heat vulnerability in Rio de Janeiro's favelas.
AI.result: Identified two distinct favela typologies: recent, well-connected settlements on flat terrain (Cluster 0) and historical, poorly-connected communities on vegetated slopes (Cluster 1). Analysis of 16 extreme heat events revealed systematic temperature differences of 2-3°C between clusters, with flat-terrain favelas experiencing significantly higher heat exposure.
AI.conclusion: Settlement morphology critically influences heat vulnerability, providing a replicable framework for targeted urban planning and public health interventions in informal settlements globally.

title: Budget-Constrained Causal Bandits: Bridging Uplift Modeling and Sequential Decision-Making
summary: Treatment allocation under budget constraints is a central challenge in digital advertising: advertisers must decide which users to show ads to while spending a limited budget wisely. The standard approach follows a two-stage offline pipeline - first collect historical data to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE), then solve a constrained optimization to allocate the budget. This works well with abundant data, but fails in cold-start settings such as new campaigns, new markets, or new customer segments where little historical data exists. We propose Budget-Constrained Causal Bandits (BCCB), an online framework that learns which users respond to ads while simultaneously spending the budget, making treatment decisions one user at a time. BCCB unifies three components into a single sequential process: learning individual-level ad effectiveness, exploring users whose response is uncertain, and pacing the budget over time. We evaluated on the Criteo Uplift dataset, a large-scale advertising dataset from a real randomized controlled trial. Our key finding is a data-efficiency crossover: offline methods require approximately 10,000 historical observations to produce reliable results, while BCCB operates effectively from the very first user. Furthermore, BCCB exhibits 3-5x lower performance variance between runs, making it more practical for real campaign planning.
