Among purely online methods, BCCB consistently outperforms standard Thompson Sampling, budgeted Thompson Sampling, and greedy HTE estimation across all budget levels tested.

AI.tldr: Budget-Constrained Causal Bandits (BCCB) - an online framework for digital advertising that learns user ad responses while spending budget efficiently, outperforming offline methods in cold-start scenarios.
AI.motivation: Standard offline methods for treatment allocation under budget constraints require abundant historical data, failing in cold-start settings like new campaigns, markets, or customer segments where little historical data exists.
AI.method: BCCB unifies three components into a single sequential process: learning individual-level ad effectiveness, exploring users with uncertain responses, and pacing budget over time, making treatment decisions one user at a time.
AI.result: Data-efficiency crossover: offline methods need ~10,000 historical observations for reliable results, while BCCB works effectively from the first user with 3-5x lower performance variance and consistently outperforms online baselines.
AI.conclusion: BCCB provides a practical solution for cold-start advertising scenarios by combining learning, exploration, and budget pacing in an online framework that requires minimal historical data.

title: Benchmarking the Safety of Large Language Models for Robotic Health Attendant Control
comment: 20 pages, 9 figures, 3 tables, 8 pages supplementary material
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly considered for deployment as the control component of robotic health attendants, yet their safety in this context remains poorly characterized. We introduce a dataset of 270 harmful instructions spanning nine prohibited behavior categories grounded in the American Medical Association Principles of Medical Ethics, and use it to evaluate 72 LLMs in a simulation environment based on the Robotic Health Attendant framework. The mean violation rate across all models was 54.4\%, with more than half exceeding 50\%, and violation rates varied substantially across behavior categories, with superficially plausible instructions such as device manipulation and emergency delay proving harder to refuse than overtly destructive ones. Model size and release date were the primary determinants of safety performance among open-weight models, and proprietary models were substantially safer than open-weight counterparts (median 23.7\% versus 72.8\%). Medical domain fine-tuning conferred no significant overall safety benefit, and a prompt-based defense strategy produced only a modest reduction in violation rates among the least safe models, leaving absolute violation rates at levels that would preclude safe clinical deployment. These findings demonstrate that safety evaluation must be treated as a first-class criterion in the development and deployment of LLMs for robotic health attendants.

AI.tldr: LLMs show alarmingly high violation rates (54.4% average) when tested against harmful instructions in robotic healthcare scenarios, with proprietary models being substantially safer than open-weight ones, but overall safety levels remain too low for clinical deployment.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly being considered for deployment in robotic healthcare systems, but their safety in this critical medical context remains poorly characterized and requires systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Researchers created a dataset of 270 harmful instructions spanning 9 prohibited behavior categories based on AMA Principles of Medical Ethics, then evaluated 72 LLMs in a Robotic Health Attendant simulation environment, analyzing violation rates across different model types, sizes, and characteristics.
AI.result: Average violation rate across all models was 54.4%, with over half of models exceeding 50%; proprietary models were substantially safer (median 23.7%) than open-weight counterparts (72.8%); medical domain fine-tuning provided no significant safety benefit; prompt-based defenses offered only modest improvements; superficially plausible harmful instructions were particularly difficult for models to refuse.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs demonstrate unacceptable safety risks for robotic healthcare applications, with violation rates at clinically dangerous levels; safety evaluation must become a first-class priority in development and deployment decisions for medical AI systems.

title: Entropy Centroids as Intrinsic Rewards for Test-Time Scaling
summary: An effective way to scale up test-time compute of large language models is to sample multiple responses and then select the best one, as in Grok Heavy and Gemini Deep Think. Existing selection methods often rely on external reward models, which requires training a strong reward model and introduces additional computation overhead. As an alternative, previous approaches have explored intrinsic signals, such as confidence and entropy, but these signals are noisy with naive aggregation. In this work, we observe that high-entropy tokens tend to cluster into consecutive groups during inference, providing a more stable notion of model uncertainty than individual tokens. Together, these clusters reveal temporal patterns of model uncertainty throughout the inference process. Motivated by this observation, we propose to use the temporal structure of uncertainty as an intrinsic reward. To this end, we first formalize the basic unit of segment-level uncertainty as the High Entropy Phase (HEP), a variable-length segment that begins at a high-entropy token and ends when consecutive low-entropy tokens appear. We then define the Entropy Centroid, inspired by the concept of the center of mass in physics, as the weighted average position of all HEPs along the trajectory. Intuitively, a lower centroid indicates early exploration followed by confident generation, which we find often corresponds to higher response quality. Based on this insight, we propose the Lowest Centroid method, which selects the response with the lowest entropy centroid among multiple candidates. Experiments on mathematics, code generation, logical reasoning, and agentic tasks, across model scales ranging from 14B to 480B, show that Lowest Centroid consistently outperforms existing baselines and delivers stable gains as model size increases. Code is available at https://github.com/hkust-nlp/entropy-centroid.

AI.tldr: Proposes "Lowest Centroid" method that uses temporal structure of uncertainty (entropy clusters) to select best responses from multiple LLM samples, outperforming existing selection methods without external reward models.
AI.motivation: Existing selection methods for scaling up test-time compute require external reward models (training overhead) or use noisy intrinsic signals like confidence/entropy. Need better intrinsic signals for response selection.
AI.method: 1) Identify High Entropy Phases (HEPs) - variable-length segments starting with high-entropy tokens until low-entropy tokens appear. 2) Define Entropy Centroid as weighted average position of all HEPs along generation trajectory. 3) Select response with lowest entropy centroid (early uncertainty followed by confident generation).
AI.result: Lowest Centroid consistently outperforms existing baselines on mathematics, code generation, logical reasoning, and agentic tasks across model scales (14B to 480B), with stable gains as model size increases.
AI.conclusion: Temporal structure of model uncertainty (via entropy centroids) provides effective intrinsic reward for response selection, eliminating need for external reward models while maintaining performance across diverse tasks and model scales.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Benchmarking of Heterogeneous LLMs for Automated Competency Assessment in Secondary Level Mathematics
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to 2AI-2026-Applied AI Conference
summary:
As Competency-Based Education (CBE) is gaining traction around the world, the shift from marks-based assessment to qualitative competency mapping is a manual challenge for educators. This paper tackles the bottleneck issue by suggesting a "Human-in-the-Loop" benchmarking framework to assess the effectiveness of multiple LLMs in automating secondary-level mathematics assessment. Based on the Grade 10 Optional Mathematics curriculum in Nepal, we created a multi-dimensional rubric for four topics and four cross-cutting competencies: Comprehension, Knowledge, Operational Fluency, and Behavior and Correlation.
  The multi-provider ensemble, consisted of open-weight models -- Eagle (Llama 3.1-8B) and Orion (Llama 3.3-70B) -- and proprietary frontier models Nova (Gemini 2.5 Flash) and Lyra (Gemini 3 Pro), was benchmarked against a ground truth defined by two senior mathematics faculty members (kappa_w = 0.8652). The findings show a marked "Architecture-compatibility gap". Although the Gemini-based Mixture-of-Experts (Sparse MoE) models achieved "Fair Agreement" (kappa_w ~ 0.38), the larger Orion (70B) model exhibited "No Agreement" (kappa_w = -0.0261), suggesting that architectural compliance with instruction constraints outweighs the scale of raw parameters in rubric-constrained tasks. We conclude that while LLMs are not yet suitable for autonomous certification, they provide high-value assistive support for preliminary evidence extraction within a "Human-in-the-Loop" framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Human-in-the-Loop framework to benchmark LLMs for automated mathematics assessment in Competency-Based Education, finding that architectural compliance with instruction constraints is more important than model size, with frontier MoE models achieving fair agreement but not yet suitable for autonomous certification.
AI.motivation: The shift from marks-based assessment to qualitative competency mapping in Competency-Based Education creates manual challenges for educators. There's a need to explore how LLMs can assist in automating secondary-level mathematics assessment within a human-in-the-loop framework.
AI.method: Created a multi-dimensional rubric based on Nepal's Grade 10 Optional Mathematics curriculum with four topics and four competencies. Benchmarked an ensemble of open-weight (Eagle/Orion) and proprietary frontier (Nova/Lyra) LLMs against expert ground truth from senior mathematics faculty. Used weighted kappa coefficient (kappa_w) for agreement measurement.
AI.result: Found an "Architecture-compatibility gap": Gemini-based Mixture-of-Experts models achieved "Fair Agreement" (kappa_w ~ 0.38) while larger Orion (70B) model showed "No Agreement" (kappa_w = -0.0261). This suggests architectural compliance with instruction constraints is more critical than parameter scale for rubric-constrained tasks.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are not yet suitable for autonomous certification but provide high-value assistive support for preliminary evidence extraction within a Human-in-the-Loop framework. The study emphasizes the importance of architectural design over raw parameter count for educational assessment tasks.

title: SWAN: World-Aware Adaptive Multimodal Networks for Runtime Variations
summary: Multimodal deep neural networks deployed in realistic environments must contend with runtime variations: changes in modality quality, overall input complexity, and available platform resources. Current networks struggle with such fluctuations -- adaptive networks cannot adhere to a strict compute budget, controller-based networks neglect to consider input complexity, and statically provisioned networks fail at all the above. Consequently, they do not extract maximum utility from the expended computational resources. We present SWAN (Sample and World-Aware Multimodal Network), the first adaptive multimodal network that accomplishes all three goals. SWAN employs a quality-aware controller to assign resources among modalities according to a variable user-specified maximum budget. Within this budget, an adaptive gating module further optimizes efficiency by scaling layer utilization according to sample complexity. For further gains, SWAN also employs a token dropping module that masks semantically irrelevant multimodal features before performing detections. We evaluate SWAN in the domain of autonomous driving with complex multi-object 3D detection, reducing FLOPs by up to 49% with minimal degradation.

AI.tldr: SWAN is an adaptive multimodal network that optimizes compute resources based on input complexity, modality quality, and user budget constraints, achieving up to 49% FLOP reduction with minimal performance degradation in autonomous driving tasks.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal networks struggle with runtime variations including changes in modality quality, input complexity, and available platform resources. They cannot efficiently adapt to these fluctuations, failing to extract maximum utility from expended computational resources.
AI.method: SWAN employs three key components: 1) a quality-aware controller that assigns resources among modalities according to user-specified budget constraints, 2) an adaptive gating module that scales layer utilization based on sample complexity, and 3) a token dropping module that masks semantically irrelevant multimodal features before detection.
AI.result: SWAN reduces FLOPs by up to 49% with minimal degradation in multi-object 3D detection performance for autonomous driving applications, outperforming existing adaptive, controller-based, and statically provisioned networks.
AI.conclusion: SWAN represents the first adaptive multimodal network that successfully addresses runtime variations in modality quality, input complexity, and resource constraints, enabling efficient computation without sacrificing performance in real-world deployment scenarios.

title: When to Vote, When to Rewrite: Disagreement-Guided Strategy Routing for Test-Time Scaling
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong performance on mathematical reasoning tasks but remain unreliable on challenging instances. Existing test-time scaling methods, such as repeated sampling, self-correction, and tree search, improve performance at the cost of increased computation, yet often exhibit diminishing returns on hard problems. We observe that output disagreement is strongly correlated with instance difficulty and prediction correctness, providing a useful signal for guiding instance-level strategy selection at test time. Based on this insight, we propose a training-free framework that formulates test-time scaling as an instance-level routing problem, rather than allocating more computation within a single strategy, dynamically selecting among different scaling strategies based on output disagreement. The framework applies lightweight resolution for consistent cases, majority voting for moderate disagreement, and rewriting-based reformulation for highly ambiguous instances. Experiments on seven mathematical benchmarks and three models show that our method improves accuracy by 3% - 7% while reducing sampling cost compared to existing approaches.

AI.tldr: A training-free framework that uses output disagreement as a signal to dynamically select between different test-time scaling strategies, improving accuracy while reducing computational cost on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models perform well on mathematical reasoning but struggle with challenging instances. Existing test-time scaling methods (repeated sampling, self-correction, tree search) improve performance but at high computational cost with diminishing returns on hard problems.
AI.method: Proposes a training-free framework that treats test-time scaling as an instance-level routing problem. Uses output disagreement (correlated with instance difficulty) to dynamically select strategies: lightweight resolution for consistent cases, majority voting for moderate disagreement, and rewriting-based reformulation for highly ambiguous instances.
AI.result: Experiments on seven mathematical benchmarks and three models show 3%-7% accuracy improvement while reducing sampling cost compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Output disagreement is an effective signal for instance-level strategy selection. The proposed training-free routing framework improves accuracy and reduces computational cost for mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Efficient and Interpretable Transformer for Counterfactual Fairness
summary: The growing reliance of machine learning models in high-stakes, highly regulated domains such as finance and insurance has created a growing tension between predictive performance, interpretability, and regulatory fairness requirements. In these settings, models are expected not only to deliver reliable predictions but also to provide transparent decision rationales and comply with strict fairness requirements. Attention-based transformers offer powerful mechanisms for modeling complex data relationships as demonstrated in various language tasks, yet their attention mechanisms alone do not ensure counterfactually fair predictions, even when combined with fairness-aware techniques. To address these limitations, we propose the Feature Correlation Transformer (FCorrTransformer), an attention-light architecture tailored for tabular data. In this design, the attention matrix admits a direct statistical interpretation as pairwise feature dependencies, enhancing both interpretability and efficiency. Leveraging this structure, we introduce Counterfactual Attention Regularization (CAR), a framework that enforces group-invariant fair representations of sensitive features at the attention level, promoting counterfactually fair predictions without relying on explicit causal assumptions. Empirical evaluations on imbalanced classification and regression benchmarks demonstrate that FCorrTransformer combined with CAR achieves strong counterfactual fairness while maintaining competitive predictive performance and substantially reducing model complexity compared with standard transformer-based baselines. Overall, this work bridges a critical gap between fairness theory and machine learning models, offering a practical framework for responsible AI in regulatory-sensitive domains.

AI.tldr: FCorrTransformer is an attention-light transformer architecture for tabular data that uses Counterfactual Attention Regularization (CAR) to achieve counterfactual fairness while maintaining interpretability and reducing complexity.
AI.motivation: There's growing tension between predictive performance, interpretability, and regulatory fairness in high-stakes domains like finance and insurance. Standard transformers don't ensure counterfactually fair predictions even with fairness-aware techniques, creating a need for models that balance all these requirements.
AI.method: Proposes FCorrTransformer, an attention-light transformer architecture for tabular data where attention matrices represent pairwise feature dependencies. Introduces Counterfactual Attention Regularization (CAR) to enforce group-invariant fair representations of sensitive features at the attention level without requiring explicit causal assumptions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on imbalanced classification and regression benchmarks show FCorrTransformer with CAR achieves strong counterfactual fairness while maintaining competitive predictive performance and substantially reducing model complexity compared to standard transformer baselines.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges fairness theory with machine learning models, offering a practical framework for responsible AI in regulatory-sensitive domains by simultaneously addressing predictive performance, interpretability, and fairness requirements.

title: SciHorizon-DataEVA: An Agentic System for AI-Readiness Evaluation of Heterogeneous Scientific Data
summary: AI-for-Science (AI4Science) is increasingly transforming scientific discovery by embedding machine learning models into prediction, simulation, and hypothesis generation workflows across domains. However, the effectiveness of these models is fundamentally constrained by the AI-readiness of scientific data, for which no scalable and systematic evaluation mechanism currently exists. In this work, we propose SciHorizon-DataEVA, a novel agentic system to scalable AI-readiness evaluation of heterogeneous scientific data. At the evaluation-criteria level, we introduce the Sci-TQA2 principles, which organize AI-readiness into four complementary dimensions: Governance Trustworthiness, Data Quality, AI Compatibility, and Scientific Adaptability. Each dimension is decomposed into measurable atomic elements that enable fine-grained and executable assessment. To operationalize these principles at scale, we develop Sci-TQA2-Eval, a hierarchical multi-agent evaluation approach orchestrated through a directed, cyclic workflow. Our Sci-TQA2-Eval dynamically constructs dataset-aware evaluation specifications by combining lightweight dataset profiling, applicability-aware metric activation, and knowledge-augmented planning grounded in domain constraints and dataset-paper signals. These specifications are executed through an adaptive, tool-centric evaluation mechanism with built-in verification and self-correction, enabling scalable and reliable assessment across heterogeneous scientific data. Extensive experiments on scientific datasets spanning multiple domains demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of SciHorizon-DataEVA for principled AI-readiness evaluation.

AI.tldr: A novel agentic system called SciHorizon-DataEVA that provides scalable AI-readiness evaluation for scientific data using the Sci-TQA2 principles and hierarchical multi-agent approach.
AI.motivation: Current AI4Science models are constrained by the AI-readiness of scientific data, but there's no scalable, systematic evaluation mechanism for heterogeneous scientific datasets.
AI.method: Introduces Sci-TQA2 principles (Governance Trustworthiness, Data Quality, AI Compatibility, Scientific Adaptability) decomposed into measurable elements. Develops Sci-TQA2-Eval, a hierarchical multi-agent evaluation approach with directed cyclic workflow that combines dataset profiling, applicability-aware metric activation, and knowledge-augmented planning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on scientific datasets across multiple domains demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of SciHorizon-DataEVA for principled AI-readiness evaluation.
AI.conclusion: SciHorizon-DataEVA provides a scalable and systematic solution for evaluating AI-readiness of scientific data, addressing a critical bottleneck in AI4Science applications.

title: Unsupervised Graph Modeling for Anomaly Detection in Accounting Subject Relationships
summary: This paper addresses the problem of anomaly detection in accounting subject association structures, proposing a structured modeling and unsupervised discriminant framework based on graph neural networks. This framework is used to mine stable correspondences between subjects and identify structural deviations from general ledger details and voucher entries. The method first abstracts accounting subjects as graph nodes, and the co-occurrence and debit/credit correspondence of subjects in the same business record are abstracted as weighted edges. The edge weights are characterized by statistical measures such as co-occurrence frequency or amount aggregation, thus forming a period-level accounting subject association graph. In the representation learning stage, a message passing mechanism is used to fuse the node's own attributes and neighborhood context to obtain node embeddings containing structural information. In the anomaly detection stage, the rationality of subject pair connections is estimated through a relation reconstruction decoder, and edge-level anomaly scores are defined based on the degree of deviation in reconstruction probabilities. These scores are then aggregated to obtain node-level risk ranking and local anomaly localization. This framework can simultaneously capture local substructure anomalies and cross-community anomaly connections without relying on anomaly labeling, outputting traceable subject pair risk clues. Comparative experiments demonstrate more stable comprehensive discriminant capabilities and higher top-ranking accuracy.

AI.tldr: A graph neural network framework for unsupervised anomaly detection in accounting subject associations, using graph representations of accounting subjects and anomaly scoring through relation reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Need to detect anomalies in accounting subject association structures without relying on labeled anomaly data, particularly to identify structural deviations from normal business transaction patterns.
AI.method: Abstracts accounting subjects as graph nodes, co-occurrence and debit/credit correspondences as weighted edges. Uses graph neural networks with message passing for node embeddings, then relation reconstruction decoder to estimate connection rationality and compute anomaly scores.
AI.result: Framework demonstrates stable comprehensive discriminant capabilities and higher top-ranking accuracy in comparative experiments, able to capture both local substructure anomalies and cross-community anomaly connections without anomaly labeling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed graph neural network-based framework effectively identifies anomalous accounting subject associations in an unsupervised manner, providing traceable risk clues and accurate anomaly detection.

title: FutureWorld: A Live Environment for Training Predictive Agents with Real-World Outcome Rewards
comment: Our experiments are ongoing, and we will release the code in the near future. We release a subset of our historical data on Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/datasets/PredictingFuture/FutureWorld
summary: Live future prediction refers to the task of making predictions about real-world events before they unfold. This task is increasingly studied using large language model-based agent systems, and it is important for building agents that can continually learn from real-world. Just as interactive environments have often driven progress in agents, advancing live future prediction naturally motivates viewing it as a learning environment. Prior works have explored future prediction from several different parts, but have generally not framed it as a unified learning environment. This task is appealing for learning because it can provide a large number of prediction questions grounded in diverse real-world events, while preventing answer leakage. To leverage the advantages of live future prediction, we present FutureWorld, a live agentic reinforcement learning environment that closes the training loop between prediction, outcome realization, and parameters update. In our environment, we take three open-source base models and train them for consecutive days. The results show that training is effective. Furthermore, we build a daily benchmark based on the environment and evaluate several frontier agents on it to establish performance baselines for current agent systems.

AI.tldr: FutureWorld is a live agentic reinforcement learning environment that treats live future prediction as a learning task, training language model-based agents on real-world event predictions and closing the loop between prediction, outcome realization, and parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Live future prediction is important for building agents that can continually learn from real-world events. However, prior works haven't framed it as a unified learning environment. The task provides numerous prediction questions grounded in diverse real-world events while preventing answer leakage, making it ideal for learning.
AI.method: The authors present FutureWorld, a live agentic reinforcement learning environment that closes the training loop between prediction, outcome realization, and parameter updates. They take three open-source base models and train them for consecutive days in this environment.
AI.result: The results show that training in the FutureWorld environment is effective. Additionally, the authors build a daily benchmark based on the environment and evaluate several frontier agents on it to establish performance baselines for current agent systems.
AI.conclusion: FutureWorld successfully demonstrates that live future prediction can be effectively framed as a learning environment, enabling agent training on real-world event predictions and establishing benchmarks for evaluating agent performance in this domain.

title: DORA: A Scalable Asynchronous Reinforcement Learning System for Language Model Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a critical paradigm for LLM post-training, yet the rollout phase -- accounting for 50--80% of total step time -- is bottlenecked by skewed generation: long-tailed trajectories indispensable for model performance block the entire training pipeline. Asynchronous training offers a natural remedy by overlapping generation with training, but introduces a fundamental tension between efficiency and algorithmic correctness. We identify three constraints in asynchronous training to preserve convergence: intra-trajectory policy consistency, data integrity, and bounded staleness. Existing approaches fail to intrinsically address the long-tailed trajectory problem, which is further exacerbated by the imbalance characteristic of Mix-of-Experts models, or deviate from the standard RL training formulation, thereby hindering model convergence. Therefore, we propose DORA (Dynamic ORchestration for Asynchronous Rollout), which addresses this challenge through algorithm-system co-design. DORA introduces multi-version streaming rollout, a novel asynchronous paradigm that maintains multiple policy versions concurrently -- simultaneously achieving full bubble elimination without compromising algorithmic constraints. Experimental results demonstrate that our DORA system achieves substantial improvements in throughput -- up to 2--3 times higher than state-of-the-art systems on open-source benchmarks -- without compromising convergence. Furthermore, in large-scale industrial applications with tens of thousands of accelerators, DORA accelerates RL training by 2--4 times compared to synchronous training across various scenarios. The resultant open-source models, LongCat-Flash-Thinking, exhibit competitive performance on complex reasoning benchmarks, matching the capability of most advanced LLMs.

AI.tldr: DORA introduces algorithm-system co-design with multi-version streaming rollout to eliminate training pipeline bubbles caused by long-tailed trajectories in RL for LLMs, achieving 2-4× speedup without compromising convergence.
AI.motivation: RL for LLMs suffers from pipeline bottlenecks where long-tailed trajectories (50-80% of training time) block entire training. Asynchronous training helps but creates tension between efficiency and algorithmic correctness. Existing approaches fail to address long-tailed trajectories properly, especially for MoE models.
AI.method: DORA uses algorithm-system co-design with multi-version streaming rollout: maintains multiple policy versions concurrently to enable full bubble elimination while preserving intra-trajectory policy consistency, data integrity, and bounded staleness constraints.
AI.result: Achieves 2-3× higher throughput than SOTA systems on open-source benchmarks; 2-4× acceleration vs synchronous training in industrial applications with tens of thousands of accelerators. Resulting models (LongCat-Flash-Thinking) show competitive performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DORA successfully resolves the efficiency-algorithmic correctness tension in asynchronous RL training for LLMs, enabling substantial speedups without convergence compromise, making it practical for large-scale industrial deployment.

title: NeuroPlastic: A Plasticity-Modulated Optimizer for Biologically Inspired Learning Dynamics
summary: Optimization algorithms are fundamental to modern deep learning, yet most widely used methods rely on update rules based primarily on local gradient statistics. We introduce NeuroPlastic, a plasticity-modulated optimizer that augments gradient-based updates with an adaptive multi-signal modulation mechanism inspired by multi-factor synaptic plasticity, a concept from neurobiology. NeuroPlastic dynamically scales gradient updates using interacting components that capture gradient, activity-like, and memory-like statistics, forming a lightweight modulation layer compatible with standard deep learning training pipelines. Across image classification benchmarks, NeuroPlastic consistently improves over a controlled gradient-only ablation, with more pronounced gains on the Fashion-MNIST benchmark and in reduced-data regimes. In transfer experiments on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, the method remains stable and competitive without retuning. These results suggest that multi-signal plasticity-inspired modulation can provide a useful extension to conventional gradient-driven optimization, particularly when learning signals are limited or noisy, and offer a promising direction for gradient-based methods in deep learning.

AI.tldr: NeuroPlastic is a plasticity-modulated optimizer that enhances gradient-based updates with adaptive multi-signal modulation inspired by neurobiological synaptic plasticity, showing consistent improvements over gradient-only methods especially in limited-data scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current optimization algorithms in deep learning rely primarily on local gradient statistics, but biological neural systems use more complex multi-factor synaptic plasticity mechanisms that could improve learning efficiency, particularly when learning signals are limited or noisy.
AI.method: NeuroPlastic augments gradient-based updates with an adaptive multi-signal modulation mechanism inspired by neurobiology, using interacting components that capture gradient, activity-like, and memory-like statistics to dynamically scale gradient updates, forming a lightweight modulation layer compatible with standard training pipelines.
AI.result: NeuroPlastic consistently outperforms gradient-only ablation across image classification benchmarks, with more pronounced gains on Fashion-MNIST and in reduced-data regimes. In CIFAR-10 transfer experiments with ResNet-18, it remains stable and competitive without retuning.
AI.conclusion: Multi-signal plasticity-inspired modulation provides a useful extension to conventional gradient-driven optimization, particularly beneficial when learning signals are limited or noisy, offering a promising direction for gradient-based methods in deep learning.

title: Cheeger--Hodge Contrastive Learning for Structurally Robust Graph Representation Learning
summary: Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) has emerged as a prominent framework for unsupervised graph representation learning. However, relying on augmentation design alone to define the invariances learned by GCL can be brittle under structural perturbations. To address this issue, we propose Cheeger--Hodge Contrastive Learning (CHCL), a framework that aligns a perturbation-stable Cheeger--Hodge joint signature across augmented views for robust graph representation learning. The proposed signature combines a Cheeger-inspired connectivity signature derived from the algebraic connectivity \(λ_2\) with the low-frequency spectrum of the 1-Hodge Laplacian, thereby capturing both global connectivity and higher-order structural information. By aligning encoder representations with the proposed Cheeger--Hodge joint signature across augmented views, CHCL learns graph embeddings that are robust to local structural perturbations. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks, transfer settings demonstrate that CHCL consistently improves performance, robustness, and generalization.

AI.tldr: Cheeger-Hodge Contrastive Learning (CHCL) is a robust graph contrastive learning framework that uses Cheeger-inspired connectivity signatures and Hodge Laplacian spectra to learn perturbation-stable graph representations by aligning joint signatures across augmented views.
AI.motivation: Current Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) frameworks rely heavily on augmentation design to define invariances, which can be brittle and unstable under structural perturbations. This limits their robustness when graphs undergo local structural changes.
AI.method: Proposes Cheeger-Hodge Contrastive Learning (CHCL) framework that aligns a perturbation-stable Cheeger-Hodge joint signature across augmented views. The signature combines: 1) a Cheeger-inspired connectivity signature derived from algebraic connectivity λ₂, and 2) the low-frequency spectrum of the 1-Hodge Laplacian to capture both global connectivity and higher-order structural information.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks and transfer settings demonstrate that CHCL consistently improves performance, robustness, and generalization compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: CHCL provides a more robust graph contrastive learning framework that mitigates the brittleness of augmentation-dependent invariances by leveraging stable Cheeger-Hodge signatures, leading to improved performance and generalization across various graph learning tasks.

title: Addressing Performance Saturation for LLM RL via Precise Entropy Curve Control
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has unlocked complex reasoning abilities in large language models (LLMs). However, most RL algorithms suffer from performance saturation, preventing further gains as RL training scales. This problem can be characterized by the collapse of entropy, a key diagnostic for exploration in RL. Existing attempts have tried to prevent entropy collapse through regularization or clipping, but their resulting entropy curves often exhibit instability in the long term, which hinders performance gains. In this paper, we introduce Entrocraft, a simple rejection-sampling approach that realizes any user-customized entropy schedule by biasing the advantage distributions. Entrocraft requires no objective regularization and is advantage-estimator-agnostic. Theoretically, we relate per-step entropy change to the advantage distribution under minimal assumptions, which explains the behavior of existing RL and entropy-preserving methods. Entrocraft also enables a systematic study of entropy schedules, where we find that linear annealing, which starts high and decays to a slightly lower target, performs best. Empirically, Entrocraft addresses performance saturation, significantly improving generalization, output diversity, and long-term training. It enables a 4B model to outperform an 8B baseline, sustains improvement for up to 4x longer before plateauing, and raises pass@K by 50% over the baseline.

AI.tldr: Entrocraft is a simple rejection-sampling method that enables custom entropy schedules in RL training to prevent entropy collapse, improving long-term performance, generalization, and output diversity in LLMs.
AI.motivation: RL training in LLMs suffers from performance saturation due to entropy collapse, which hinders exploration. Existing methods using regularization or clipping show unstable entropy curves over long training periods, limiting further performance gains.
AI.method: Entrocraft uses a rejection-sampling approach to implement any user-customized entropy schedule by biasing the advantage distributions. It requires no objective regularization and is advantage-estimator-agnostic.
AI.result: The method addresses performance saturation, significantly improving generalization, output diversity, and long-term training. It enables a 4B model to outperform an 8B baseline, sustains improvement 4x longer before plateauing, and raises pass@K by 50%.
AI.conclusion: Entrocraft effectively prevents entropy collapse in RL training, enables systematic study of entropy schedules (finding linear annealing works best), and provides stable long-term performance improvements without the instability issues of existing methods.

title: AlphaJet: Automated Conceptual Aircraft Synthesis via Disentangled Generative Priors and Topology-Preserving Evolutionary Search
summary: Conceptual aircraft design is traditionally an expert-mediated iterative process in which a human designer proposes a configuration, runs low-order physics, inspects the result, and re-proposes. We present AlphaJet, an end-to-end automated synthesis pipeline that closes this loop. From a textual mission specification (mass, range, cruise speed, hard size envelope, engine count, areal density) AlphaJet evolves a feasible 3D aircraft in real time, scored by a transparent multi-disciplinary fitness function covering aerodynamics, structures, weights, stability, packaging, and geometric mount consistency. Three contributions distinguish our approach: (i) an Anatomically-Disentangled Variational Autoencoder (AD-VAE) whose first 25 latent dimensions are supervised to align with named anatomical parameters, providing an interpretable shape prior; (ii) a topology-elitist genetic algorithm that protects the best individual from each of five tail topologies and triggers stagnation restarts, preventing premature collapse to a single configuration; and (iii) mount-aware geometric scoring that computes signed penetration between engines and other structural parts, eliminating the redundant artifacts common in generative aircraft models. The full loop runs interactively on a CPU and streams every generation to a browser viewer, making it a practical real-world automation tool for early-phase design-space exploration.

AI.tldr: AlphaJet is an end-to-end automated aircraft design synthesis pipeline that evolves feasible 3D aircraft from textual mission specs using interpretable latent representations, genetic algorithms, and geometric consistency scoring.
AI.motivation: Traditional conceptual aircraft design is an expert-mediated iterative process requiring human intervention to propose configurations, run analysis, inspect results, and refine designs. This manual loop is time-consuming and limited by human cognitive capacity for exploring design spaces.
AI.method: 1) Anatomically-Disentangled VAE with first 25 latent dimensions supervised to align with named anatomical parameters for interpretable shape prior; 2) Topology-elitist genetic algorithm that protects best individuals from five tail topologies and triggers stagnation restarts to avoid premature convergence; 3) Mount-aware geometric scoring that computes signed penetration between engines and structural parts to eliminate unrealistic artifacts.
AI.result: The system evolves feasible 3D aircraft in real time from textual mission specifications, scored by a transparent multi-disciplinary fitness function covering aerodynamics, structures, weights, stability, packaging, and geometric mount consistency. The full loop runs interactively on CPU and streams to browser.
AI.conclusion: AlphaJet presents a practical real-world automation tool for early-phase design-space exploration that closes the aircraft design loop without requiring expert mediation at each iteration, enabling rapid exploration of feasible configurations.

title: Adaptive and Fine-grained Module-wise Expert Pruning for Efficient LoRA-MoE Fine-Tuning
summary: LoRA-MoE has emerged as an effective paradigm for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, combining the low training cost of LoRA with the increased adaptation capacity of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE). However, existing LoRA-MoE frameworks typically adopt a fixed and uniform expert configuration across heterogeneous Transformer modules (\eg, attention query/key projections and MLP gating networks), ignoring their distinct functional roles and capacity requirements. This design leads to localized over-provisioning, redundant trainable parameters, and unnecessary optimizer-state overhead. Moreover, prior methods enforce load balancing among experts throughout training. Although beneficial in the early stage, this constraint becomes restrictive once routing patterns stabilize, limiting expert specialization on downstream tasks. In this paper, we propose DMEP, a novel LoRA-MoE fine-tuning framework based on Dynamic Module-wise Expert Pruning. DMEP tracks expert utilization during training and physically removes low-utility experts on a per-module basis, yielding a more compact expert structure tailored to different modules. The pruned model then continues training without the load-balancing constraint, freeing the remaining experts to focus entirely on the downstream task and develop specialized expertise. By jointly adapting module-wise expert capacity and eliminating unnecessary balancing, DMEP improves both parameter efficiency and training efficiency. Extensive experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks show that DMEP reduces trainable parameters by 35\%--43\% and improves training throughput by about 10\%, while maintaining or surpassing the downstream reasoning accuracy of uniform LoRA-MoE baselines.

AI.tldr: DMEP is a LoRA-MoE fine-tuning framework that dynamically prunes low-utility experts per module to create tailored expert structures, improving parameter and training efficiency while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LoRA-MoE frameworks use fixed, uniform expert configurations across heterogeneous Transformer modules, ignoring their distinct functional roles and capacity requirements. This leads to localized over-provisioning, redundant parameters, and optimizer overhead. Additionally, load balancing constraints become restrictive after routing patterns stabilize, limiting expert specialization.
AI.method: DMEP tracks expert utilization during training and physically removes low-utility experts on a per-module basis, creating tailored expert structures. The pruned model continues training without load-balancing constraints, allowing remaining experts to specialize on downstream tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks show DMEP reduces trainable parameters by 35-43% and improves training throughput by about 10%, while maintaining or surpassing the downstream reasoning accuracy of uniform LoRA-MoE baselines.
AI.conclusion: DMEP addresses key limitations of uniform LoRA-MoE frameworks by jointly adapting module-wise expert capacity and eliminating unnecessary balancing, resulting in improved parameter efficiency and training efficiency for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Discounting for Mitigating Reward Hacking
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) systems typically optimize scalar reward functions that assume precise and reliable evaluation of outcomes. However, real-world objectives--especially those derived from human preferences--are often uncertain, context-dependent, and internally inconsistent. This mismatch can lead to alignment failures such as reward hacking, over-optimization, and overconfident behavior.
  We introduce a dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework that explicitly models both epistemic uncertainty in value estimation and uncertainty in human preferences. Model uncertainty is captured via ensemble disagreement over value predictions, while preference uncertainty is derived from variability in reward annotations. We combine these signals through a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that adaptively modulates action selection, encouraging a balance between exploitation and caution.
  Empirical results across multiple discrete grid configurations (6x6, 8x8, 10x10) and high-dimensional continuous control environments (Hopper-v4, Walker2d-v4) demonstrate that our approach yields more stable training dynamics and reduces exploitative behaviors under reward ambiguity, achieving a 93.7% reduction in reward-hacking behavior as measured by trap visitation frequency. We demonstrate statistical significance of these improvements and robustness under up to 30% supervisory noise, albeit with a trade-off in peak observed reward compared to unconstrained baselines.
  By treating uncertainty as a first-class component of the reward signal, this work offers a principled approach toward more reliable and aligned reinforcement learning systems.

AI.tldr: A dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework for RL that models both epistemic uncertainty and human preference uncertainty, reducing reward hacking by 93.7% through adaptive confidence-based action selection.
AI.motivation: Real-world RL objectives, especially those from human preferences, are often uncertain, context-dependent, and inconsistent, leading to alignment failures like reward hacking, over-optimization, and overconfident behavior.
AI.method: Introduced a dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework modeling epistemic uncertainty (via ensemble disagreement over value predictions) and preference uncertainty (from variability in reward annotations), combined through a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that adaptively modulates action selection.
AI.result: Demonstrated 93.7% reduction in reward-hacking behavior measured by trap visitation frequency across multiple discrete grid environments (6x6, 8x8, 10x10) and high-dimensional continuous control environments (Hopper-v4, Walker2d-v4), with statistical significance and robustness under up to 30% supervisory noise, though with trade-off in peak observed reward.
AI.conclusion: By treating uncertainty as a first-class component of the reward signal, this work offers a principled approach toward more reliable and aligned reinforcement learning systems.

title: CoQuant: Joint Weight-Activation Subspace Projection for Mixed-Precision LLMs
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) has become an important technique for reducing the inference cost of Large Language Models (LLMs). While recent mixed-precision methods improve ultra-low bit quantization by preserving critical subspaces in high precision, they typically construct these subspaces relying solely on activation statistics. This ignores the fundamental nature of linear operations, where the output perturbation is jointly driven by both activation and weight quantization noise. In this paper, we propose CoQuant, a joint weight-activation subspace projection method. By theoretically modeling the expected output error, CoQuant formulates a closed-form weighted PCA solution that balances activation and weight covariances to select the optimal high-precision subspace. Extensive experiments on Llama-3.2 and Qwen2.5 models show that CoQuant consistently outperforms strong PTQ baselines in both WikiText perplexity and zero-shot common-sense reasoning accuracy. These results demonstrate that joint weight-activation subspace modeling provides a principled and effective direction for low-bit LLM quantization. The source code is available at https://github.com/Zachary5895/CoQuant.

AI.tldr: CoQuant is a joint weight-activation subspace projection method for LLM quantization that outperforms existing PTQ methods by modeling output error from both weight and activation quantization noise.
AI.motivation: Existing mixed-precision quantization methods for LLMs only consider activation statistics when selecting high-precision subspaces, ignoring that output perturbations in linear operations come from both weight AND activation quantization noise. This fundamental limitation reduces quantization effectiveness.
AI.method: CoQuant proposes a joint weight-activation subspace projection method that theoretically models expected output error. It formulates a closed-form weighted PCA solution that balances activation and weight covariances to select optimal high-precision subspaces.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Llama-3.2 and Qwen2.5 models show CoQuant consistently outperforms strong PTQ baselines in both WikiText perplexity and zero-shot common-sense reasoning accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Joint weight-activation subspace modeling provides a principled and effective direction for low-bit LLM quantization, demonstrating superior performance over activation-only approaches.

title: Unifying Runtime Monitoring Approaches for Safety-Critical Machine Learning: Application to Vision-Based Landing
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables, submitted to ICPR 2026
summary: Runtime monitoring is essential to ensure the safety of ML applications in safety-critical domains. However, current research is fragmented, with independent methods emerging from different communities. In this paper, we propose a unified framework categorising runtime monitoring approaches into three distinct types: Operational Design Domain (ODD) monitoring, which ensures compliance with expected operating conditions; Out-of-Distribution (OOD) monitoring, which rejects inputs that deviate from the training data; and Out-of-Model-Scope (OMS) monitoring, which detects anomalous model behaviour based its internal states or outputs. We demonstrate the benefits of this categorization with a dedicated experiment on an aeronautical safety-critical application: runway detection during landing. This framework facilitates design of monitoring activities, with complementary categories of monitors, and enables evaluation and comparison of different monitors using common, safety-oriented metrics.

AI.tldr: A unified framework categorizes runtime monitoring approaches for ML safety into three types: Operational Design Domain (ODD), Out-of-Distribution (OOD), and Out-of-Model-Scope (OMS) monitoring.
AI.motivation: Current research on runtime monitoring for ML safety is fragmented with independent methods emerging from different communities, creating a need for a unified framework to organize and compare approaches.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified categorization framework dividing runtime monitoring into three distinct types: ODD monitoring (ensuring compliance with operating conditions), OOD monitoring (rejecting inputs deviating from training data), and OMS monitoring (detecting anomalous model behavior based on internal states/outputs).
AI.result: The framework is demonstrated through experiments on an aeronautical safety-critical application (runway detection during landing), showing that the categorization facilitates monitor design with complementary categories and enables evaluation using common safety-oriented metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified framework provides a structured way to design runtime monitoring activities, compare different monitoring approaches, and enhance ML safety in critical applications through complementary monitoring categories.

title: STLGT: A Scalable Trace-Based Linear Graph Transformer for Tail Latency Prediction in Microservices
summary: Accurate end-to-end tail-latency forecasting is critical for proactive SLO management in microservice systems. However, modeling long-range dependency propagation and non-stationary, bursty workloads while maintaining inference efficiency at scale remains challenging. We present STLGT (Scalable Trace-based Linear Graph Transformer), a per-API predictor that encodes traces as span graphs for multi-step p95 tail-latency forecasting. STLGT uses a structure-aware linear graph Transformer to propagate cross-service dependencies with inference time linear in span graph size, and a decoupled temporal module to capture workload dynamics. Across a personalized education microservice application, DeathStarBench, and Alibaba traces, STLGT improves forecasting accuracy over PERT-GNN by 8.5% MAPE on average and achieves up to 12x faster CPU inference at N=32, matching the maximum span graph size after preprocessing the Alibaba traces. Ablation studies further demonstrate the effectiveness of each component, especially under bursty traffic.

AI.tldr: STLGT is a scalable trace-based linear graph Transformer for end-to-end tail-latency forecasting in microservices that achieves 8.5% better accuracy and up to 12x faster inference than previous methods.
AI.motivation: Accurate end-to-end tail-latency forecasting is critical for proactive SLO management in microservices, but current methods struggle with modeling long-range dependency propagation, non-stationary bursty workloads, and maintaining inference efficiency at scale.
AI.method: STLGT encodes traces as span graphs for multi-step p95 tail-latency forecasting using a structure-aware linear graph Transformer for cross-service dependency propagation (linear inference time) and a decoupled temporal module for workload dynamics.
AI.result: Across three benchmarks (personalized education app, DeathStarBench, Alibaba traces), STLGT improves forecasting accuracy over PERT-GNN by 8.5% MAPE on average and achieves up to 12x faster CPU inference at N=32 (maximum span graph size after Alibaba trace preprocessing).
AI.conclusion: STLGT effectively addresses tail-latency forecasting challenges in microservices with improved accuracy and efficiency, especially under bursty traffic, making it suitable for proactive SLO management at scale.

title: Layer-wise Lipschitz-Product Control for Deep Kolmogorov--Arnold Network Representations of Compositionally Structured Functions
comment: 15 pages, theoretical note on layer-wise Lipschitz control for deep KANs
summary: We prove that any continuous function f from [0,1]^n to R representable by a finite computation tree with N internal nodes and compositional sparsity s = O(1) admits a deep Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) representation. Each internal node is realised by a primitive KAN block with controlled block depth and Lipschitz product. The layer-wise Lipschitz product satisfies the primary domain-sensitive bound independent of the input dimension n. It simplifies to P(KAN_f) <= max(C*,1)^L_f with L_f <= c_max * N. For the standard operations {+,-,x,sin,cos} with x nodes on [0,1]-bounded inputs we obtain P(KAN) <= 1. Layer widths satisfy n_l <= n + 2 w_max * N. The uniform approximation error is bounded by N * max(C*,1)^d(f) * epsilon_Op (simplifies when C* <=1). For f in C^m we obtain optimal B-spline rates. Range bounds are also derived (B_f <= N+1 for additive trees). This addresses the gap on Lipschitz control in deep KAN stacks noted by Liu et al. (2024). Experiments confirm P(KAN)=1.0 for several compositionally structured functions.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that any continuous function representable by a finite computation tree can be represented by deep Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with controlled Lipschitz constants and optimal approximation rates, addressing a gap in Lipschitz control for deep KAN stacks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the gap in Lipschitz control in deep KAN stacks noted by Liu et al. (2024), providing theoretical guarantees for the Lipschitz properties of deep KAN representations of compositionally structured functions.
AI.method: The method proves that functions representable by finite computation trees with compositional sparsity can be represented by deep KANs. Each internal node is realized by a primitive KAN block with controlled block depth and Lipschitz product, achieving domain-sensitive bounds independent of input dimension.
AI.result: Key results include: 1) Layer-wise Lipschitz product bounds independent of input dimension, 2) Width bounds scaling as n + 2w_max*N, 3) Uniform approximation error bounds, 4) Optimal B-spline rates for C^m functions, 5) Experimental confirmation that P(KAN)=1.0 for compositionally structured functions.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully provides theoretical foundations for Lipschitz control in deep KAN representations, showing that compositionally sparse functions can be efficiently approximated by KANs with controlled complexity and Lipschitz properties, with practical experimental validation.

title: Near-Optimal Cryptographic Hardness of Learning With Homogeneous Halfspaces Under Gaussian Marginals
summary: We study three problems that involve identifying homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian distributions: agnostic learning, one-sided reliable learning, and fairness auditing. In each of these problems, we are given labeled examples $(\mathbf{x}, \mathrm{y})$ drawn from an unknown distribution on $\mathbb{R}^d\times\{-1, +1\}$, whose marginal distribution on $\mathbf{x}$ is standard Gaussian and on $\mathrm{y}$ is arbitrary. The goal of each problem is to output a homogeneous halfspace that approaches the best-fitting homogeneous halfspace in terms of its corresponding loss measure. We prove near-optimal computational hardness results for these problems under the widely believed hardness assumption of the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem. Prior hardness results for these problems were mostly established for general halfspaces; our findings extend some of these hardness results to homogeneous halfspaces. Remarkably, our lower bound strictly generalizes over prior works and narrows the gap between the upper and lower bounds for agnostically learning homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian marginals.

AI.tldr: The paper proves near-optimal computational hardness results for three problems involving homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian distributions: agnostic learning, one-sided reliable learning, and fairness auditing, assuming the Learning With Errors (LWE) hardness assumption.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to extend prior hardness results for general halfspaces to homogeneous halfspaces, particularly narrowing the gap between upper and lower bounds for agnostically learning homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian marginals. The researchers want to understand the computational limits of these important learning and fairness problems.
AI.method: The authors study three specific problems involving homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian distributions. They prove computational hardness results by establishing reductions based on the widely believed hardness assumption of the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem. The method involves theoretical analysis of these learning problems under the constraint that the input distribution's marginal on x is standard Gaussian and on y is arbitrary.
AI.result: The paper proves near-optimal computational hardness results for: (1) agnostic learning of homogeneous halfspaces, (2) one-sided reliable learning, and (3) fairness auditing. The lower bounds strictly generalize over prior works and significantly narrow the gap between upper and lower bounds for agnostically learning homogeneous halfspaces under Gaussian marginals.
AI.conclusion: The research successfully extends prior hardness results from general halfspaces to homogeneous halfspaces, providing strong evidence for the computational intractability of these problems under standard cryptographic assumptions. The findings have implications for understanding the fundamental limits of learning algorithms and fairness auditing procedures in machine learning.

title: Hierarchical adaptive control for real-time dynamic inference at the edge
comment: Accepted as paper at 5th Real-time And intelliGent Edge computing (RAGE 2026) workshop
summary: Industrial systems increasingly depend on Machine Learning (ML), and operate on heterogeneous nodes that must satisfy tight latency, energy, and memory constraints. Dynamic ML models, which reconfigure their computational footprint at runtime, promise high energy efficiency and lower average latency for modest accuracy tradeoffs; however, their deployment is complex due to the additional hyperparameters they rely on. These hyperparameters, controlling the accuracy versus average latency tradeoff, are often tuned on a calibration dataset that must match the test time distribution, an assumption that rarely holds in real-world scenarios, leading to suboptimal operational conditions, possibly below static models. We propose a two-tier adaptive architecture that co-optimizes model and system decisions. At the global level, a scheduler configures and deploys, for each edge node, a cascade of classifiers composed of lightweight specialized models and a generalist fallback, satisfying latency and memory constraints. At the node level, a local controller tracks data drifts and hardware resources, enabling or disabling specialized predictors (SP) to preserve high energy efficiency and avoid latency-constraint violations under varying conditions. This design allows longer operating times without forcing a global redeployment step, and enables efficient execution in case of an unreachable remote global controller. We evaluate the approach on two datasets under controlled distribution mismatch scenarios, showing average per-inference reductions of latency up to 2.45x and energy up to 2.86x, with less than 4% accuracy drop compared to static baselines. Our contributions are:(1) a budgeted SP-cascade formulation that preserves worst-case latency constraints;(2) a hierarchical controller that maintains efficiency under data and resource changes; and (3) an experimental evaluation on embedded hardware.

AI.tldr: Two-tier adaptive ML system for edge devices that dynamically adjusts model complexity based on data drift and resource constraints to maintain efficiency without global redeployment.
AI.motivation: Current dynamic ML models have complex hyperparameters that require calibration matching test distribution, which rarely holds in real-world edge scenarios, leading to suboptimal performance compared to static models.
AI.method: Two-tier architecture: global scheduler configures classifier cascades (lightweight specialized models + generalist fallback) for each edge node; local controller tracks data drifts and hardware resources to enable/disable specialized predictors while preserving constraints.
AI.result: Evaluation shows 2.45x latency reduction, 2.86x energy reduction, with <4% accuracy drop compared to static baselines under distribution mismatch scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical adaptive approach enables efficient ML execution on edge devices under changing conditions without requiring global redeployment, maintaining worst-case latency constraints and high energy efficiency.

title: Understanding DNNs in Feature Interaction Models: A Dimensional Collapse Perspective
summary: DNNs have gained widespread adoption in feature interaction recommendation models. However, there has been a longstanding debate on their roles. On one hand, some works claim that DNNs possess the ability to implicitly capture high-order feature interactions. Conversely, recent studies have highlighted the limitations of DNNs in effectively learning dot products, specifically second-order interactions, let alone higher-order interactions. In this paper, we present a novel perspective to understand the effectiveness of DNNs: their impact on the dimensional robustness of the representations. In particular, we conduct extensive experiments involving both parallel DNNs and stacked DNNs. Our evaluation encompasses an overall study of complete DNN on two feature interaction models, alongside a fine-grained ablation analysis of components within DNNs. Experimental results demonstrate that both parallel and stacked DNNs can effectively mitigate the dimensional collapse of embeddings. Furthermore, a gradient-based theoretical analysis, supported by empirical evidence, uncovers the underlying mechanisms of dimensional collapse.

AI.tldr: DNNs in recommendation models improve feature interaction learning by enhancing dimensional robustness of embeddings, preventing dimensional collapse.
AI.motivation: There's conflicting evidence about DNNs' ability to capture feature interactions in recommendation systems - some claim they can learn high-order interactions while others argue they struggle with even second-order interactions, creating a need to better understand DNNs' actual contribution.
AI.method: Conducted extensive experiments with parallel and stacked DNNs, including overall studies of complete DNNs on two feature interaction models and fine-grained ablation analysis of DNN components, complemented by gradient-based theoretical analysis.
AI.result: Both parallel and stacked DNNs effectively mitigate dimensional collapse of embeddings, and the theoretical analysis reveals the underlying mechanisms of how DNNs prevent this collapse.
AI.conclusion: DNNs' effectiveness in recommendation models stems not from capturing high-order interactions per se, but from enhancing the dimensional robustness of representations, preventing dimensional collapse and improving overall model performance.

title: Do Larger Models Really Win in Drug Discovery? A Benchmark Assessment of Model Scaling in AI-Driven Molecular Property and Activity Prediction
summary: The rapid growth of molecular foundation models and general-purpose large language models has encouraged a scale-centric view of artificial intelligence in drug discovery, in which larger pretrained models are expected to supersede compact cheminformatics models and task-specific graph neural networks (GNNs). We test this assumption on 22 molecular property and activity endpoints, including public ADMET and Tox21 benchmarks and two internal anti-infective activity datasets. Across 167,056 held-out task--molecule evaluations under structure-similarity-separated five-fold cross-validation (37,756 ADMET, 77,946 Tox21, 49,266 anti-TB and 2,088 antimalaria), classical machine-learning (ML) models such as RF(ECFP4) and ExtraTrees(RDKit descriptors) win ten primary-metric tasks, GNNs such as GIN and Ligandformer win nine, and pretrained molecular sequence models such as MoLFormer and ChemBERTa2 win three. Rule-based SAR reasoning baselines, represented by GPT5.5-SAR and Opus4.7-SAR, do not win under the prespecified primary metrics, although train-fold-derived SAR knowledge provides measurable but uneven gains for SAR reasoning and interpretation. These results indicate that compact, specialized models remain highly effective for molecular property and activity prediction. The performance differences among classical ML, GNN and pretrained sequence models are often modest and endpoint-dependent, whereas larger or more general models do not provide a universal predictive advantage. Large models may still add value for zero-shot reasoning, SAR interpretation and hypothesis generation, but the results suggest that predictive performance depends on the alignment among molecular representation, inductive bias, data regime, endpoint biology and validation protocol.

AI.tldr: Large foundation models don't automatically outperform specialized cheminformatics models in molecular property prediction; classical ML and GNNs still win most tasks against pretrained sequence models in benchmark studies.
AI.motivation: To test whether larger pretrained molecular foundation models and general-purpose LLMs provide universal predictive advantages over compact, specialized cheminformatics models and task-specific GNNs for molecular property and activity prediction.
AI.method: Systematic benchmarking on 22 molecular property endpoints using structure-similarity-separated five-fold cross-validation across public ADMET/Tox21 benchmarks and internal anti-infective datasets, totaling 167,056 evaluations. Compared classical ML (RF/ExtraTrees), GNNs (GIN/Ligandformer), pretrained sequence models (MoLFormer/ChemBERTa2), and rule-based SAR reasoning baselines using primary metrics.
AI.result: Classical ML models won 10 tasks, GNNs won 9 tasks, and pretrained sequence models won only 3 tasks. Rule-based SAR baselines didn't win under primary metrics. Performance differences were modest and endpoint-dependent, with no universal advantage for larger/general models.
AI.conclusion: Compact, specialized models remain highly effective for molecular property prediction. Performance depends on alignment among molecular representation, inductive bias, data regime, endpoint biology, and validation protocol rather than model size alone. Large models may add value for zero-shot reasoning and interpretation, but predictive superiority isn't guaranteed.

title: Progressive Semantic Communication for Efficient Edge-Cloud Vision-Language Models
comment: Under review. Extended version with additional figures and appendices
summary: Deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on edge devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational and memory demands, which exceed the capabilities of resource-constrained embedded platforms. Conversely, fully offloading inference to the cloud is often impractical in bandwidth-limited environments, where transmitting raw visual data introduces substantial latency overhead. While recent edge-cloud collaborative architectures attempt to partition VLM workloads across devices, they typically rely on transmitting fixed-size representations, lacking adaptability to dynamic network conditions and failing to fully exploit semantic redundancy. In this paper, we propose a progressive semantic communication framework for edge-cloud VLM inference, using a Meta AutoEncoder that compresses visual tokens into adaptive, progressively refinable representations, enabling plug-and-play deployment with off-the-shelf VLMs without additional fine-tuning. This design allows flexible transmission at different information levels, providing a controllable trade-off between communication cost and semantic fidelity. We implement a full end-to-end edge-cloud system comprising an embedded NXP i.MX95 platform and a GPU server, communicating over bandwidth-constrained networks. Experimental results show that, at 1 Mbps uplink, the proposed progressive scheme significantly reduces network latency compared to full-edge and full-cloud solutions, while maintaining high semantic consistency even under high compression. The implementation code will be released upon publication at https://github.com/open-ep/ProSemComVLM.

AI.tldr: A progressive semantic communication framework for edge-cloud VLM inference that compresses visual tokens into adaptive representations, enabling plug-and-play deployment without fine-tuning while optimizing latency and semantic fidelity under bandwidth constraints.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have high computational demands that exceed edge device capabilities, while full cloud offloading suffers from latency in bandwidth-limited environments due to raw data transmission. Existing edge-cloud collaborative approaches lack adaptability to dynamic network conditions and don't fully exploit semantic redundancy.
AI.method: Proposes a progressive semantic communication framework using a Meta AutoEncoder to compress visual tokens into adaptive, progressively refinable representations. The system enables plug-and-play deployment with off-the-shelf VLMs without additional fine-tuning, allowing flexible transmission at different information levels to balance communication cost and semantic fidelity.
AI.result: Implemented a full end-to-end edge-cloud system on embedded NXP i.MX95 platform and GPU server. At 1 Mbps uplink, the progressive scheme significantly reduces network latency compared to full-edge and full-cloud solutions while maintaining high semantic consistency even under high compression.
AI.conclusion: The proposed progressive semantic communication framework effectively addresses the challenge of deploying VLMs on resource-constrained edge devices by providing an adaptable solution that optimizes the trade-off between communication cost and semantic fidelity without requiring VLM modifications.

title: Lyapunov-Guided Self-Alignment: Test-Time Adaptation for Offline Safe Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at AISTATS 2026. First two authors contributed equally. Project page: https://seungyubhan.github.io/sas/. Code: https://github.com/seungyubhan/sas
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) agents often fail when deployed, as the gap between training datasets and real environments leads to unsafe behavior. To address this, we present SAS (Self-Alignment for Safety), a transformer-based framework that enables test-time adaptation in offline safe RL without retraining. In SAS, the main mechanism is self-alignment: at test time, the pretrained agent generates several imagined trajectories and selects those satisfying the Lyapunov condition. These feasible segments are then recycled as in-context prompts, allowing the agent to realign its behavior toward safety while avoiding parameter updates. In effect, SAS turns Lyapunov-guided imagination into control-invariant prompts, and its transformer architecture admits a hierarchical RL interpretation where prompting functions as Bayesian inference over latent skills. Across Safety Gymnasium and MuJoCo benchmarks, SAS consistently reduces cost and failure while maintaining or improving return.

AI.tldr: SAS is a transformer-based framework that enables test-time safety adaptation in offline RL without retraining, using self-alignment through imagined trajectories and Lyapunov-guided prompting.
AI.motivation: Offline RL agents often fail when deployed due to gaps between training datasets and real environments, leading to unsafe behavior. Existing methods require retraining to adapt to new safety constraints at test time, which is computationally expensive and impractical.
AI.method: SAS uses a transformer-based framework with self-alignment: at test time, the pretrained agent generates multiple imagined trajectories, selects those satisfying the Lyapunov safety condition, and recycles these feasible segments as in-context prompts. This allows real-time adaptation without parameter updates, effectively turning Lyapunov-guided imagination into control-invariant prompts.
AI.result: Across Safety Gymnasium and MuJoCo benchmarks, SAS consistently reduces cost and failure rates while maintaining or improving task return compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: SAS provides an effective framework for test-time safety adaptation in offline RL without retraining, using self-alignment through in-context prompting and Lyapunov conditions to bridge the gap between training data and deployment environments.
